title: Euler Scheme for Stochastic Functional Differential Equations Driven by Fractional Brownian Motion via Fractional Calculus Techniques
summary: We study a stochastic functional differential equation (SFDE) with memory driven by a fractional Brownian motion (fBm) with Hurst parameter H>1/2. An Euler-type numerical scheme is proposed and analyzed under suitable regularity conditions on the drift and diffusion coefficients using tools from fractional calculus. We prove the convergence of the scheme and derive the corresponding rate in terms of the discretization step. Numerical simulations illustrate the theoretical results and confirm the accuracy of the proposed method.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes and analyzes an Euler-type numerical scheme for stochastic functional differential equations with memory driven by fractional Brownian motion (Hurst parameter H > 1/2), proving convergence rates and validating results through simulations.
AI.motivation: To develop and analyze numerical methods for SFDEs with memory driven by fBm, which have applications in modeling systems with long-range dependence and memory effects.
AI.method: An Euler-type numerical scheme is proposed and analyzed using tools from fractional calculus, under suitable regularity conditions on drift and diffusion coefficients.
AI.result: Convergence of the scheme is proved, and the corresponding convergence rate in terms of the discretization step is derived. Numerical simulations confirm theoretical findings and method accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Euler scheme converges with a quantified rate for SFDEs driven by fBm with H > 1/2, supported by both theoretical analysis and numerical validation.

title: Entropy-Compatible Barrier Schemes for Diffusive FENE Flows
summary: FENE-type conformation-tensor models impose a finite-extensibility constraint that is absent from Oldroyd--B flow: the conformation tensor must satisfy $\CC\succ0$ and $\tr\CC<L^2$. Positive definiteness alone is therefore insufficient, since a numerical state can remain positive while crossing the singular trace barrier. Even a trace-preserving logarithmic parametrization is not enough by itself: high-order reconstruction can remain inside the finite-extensibility domain while injecting artificial FENE entropy. We develop and analyze a barrier-preserving entropy-compatible discretization for FENE-P type flows with polymer center-of-mass molecular diffusion and for trace-singular FENE-family closures with the same entropy structure. The method combines a trace-barrier free energy, a finite-extensibility logarithmic parametrization, a least-damping entropy-compatible barrier-log reconstruction, molecular diffusion paired with the barrier entropy variable, compatible quadrature for polymeric work, and a scaled FENE stress variable for the small-Weissenberg limit. For admissible discrete states we prove finite-extensibility preservation at entropy quadrature points, existence and bisection computability of the maximal entropy-admissible reconstruction parameter, a fully discrete free-energy inequality with relaxation and molecular-diffusion barrier dissipation, a quantitative AP stress closure, and a fixed-discretization Newtonian limit. A conditional relative-entropy estimate is derived on compact subsets of the finite-extensibility domain. Numerical diagnostics verify barrier preservation, entropy-compatible reconstruction, energy decay, AP closure, coupled velocity--pressure--stress accuracy, and high-Weissenberg robustness near the trace constraint.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a barrier-preserving, entropy-compatible numerical method for FENE-type polymer flow models that maintains the finite-extensibility constraint (tr C < L²), prevents artificial entropy injection, and proves key stability, accuracy, and robustness properties.
AI.motivation: Standard Oldroyd-B models lack finite-extensibility constraints. FENE-type models require tr C < L², but positive definiteness alone is insufficient—numerical states can cross the singular trace barrier. Existing logarithmic parametrizations don't fully prevent artificial entropy injection during high-order reconstruction.
AI.method: Combines a trace-barrier free energy, finite-extensibility logarithmic parametrization, least-damping entropy-compatible barrier-log reconstruction, molecular diffusion paired with barrier entropy variable, compatible quadrature for polymeric work, and a scaled FENE stress variable for small-Weissenberg limits.
AI.result: Proves finite-extensibility preservation at entropy quadrature points, existence of maximal entropy-admissible reconstruction parameter, discrete free-energy inequality with relaxation and barrier dissipation, quantitative AP stress closure, Newtonian limit, and conditional relative-entropy estimate. Numerical diagnostics confirm all properties.
AI.conclusion: The proposed discretization is provably stable, entropy-compatible, and robust for high-Weissenberg FENE-P flows near the trace constraint, with rigorous theoretical guarantees and numerical verification.

title: Optimizing Irreversible Perturbations of the Unadjusted Langevin Algorithm
summary: Irreversible perturbations accelerate the convergence of Langevin dynamics, breaking detailed balance while preserving the invariant measure. The design of optimal irreversible perturbations has been studied in the continuous-time Gaussian setting, but extensions to non-Gaussian target distributions, and the impact of time discretization on the design of optimal perturbations, have not been well understood. Numerical discretizations of Langevin dynamics introduce bias, which is typically exacerbated by irreversible perturbations; handling this interaction demands a joint treatment of acceleration and accuracy. This paper develops a systematic framework for optimizing position-independent irreversible perturbations of the unadjusted Langevin algorithm (ULA). We formulate a constrained optimization problem that simultaneously accounts for mixing efficiency and discretization bias, where the former is characterized by a spectral gap analogue and the latter is quantified via a weighted expected squared jump distance. Within this framework, we derive an explicit characterization of the optimal position-independent irreversible perturbation. Extensive numerical experiments demonstrate that our design yields faster convergence with controlled bias, and improves mean squared estimation errors compared to other choices of irreversible perturbation.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a systematic framework for optimizing position-independent irreversible perturbations of the unadjusted Langevin algorithm (ULA) to accelerate convergence while controlling discretization bias, deriving explicit optimal perturbations and demonstrating improved performance through numerical experiments.
AI.motivation: Optimal irreversible perturbations have been studied in continuous-time Gaussian settings, but extensions to non-Gaussian targets and the impact of time discretization on optimal perturbation design are not well understood.
AI.method: Formulates a constrained optimization problem balancing mixing efficiency (via a spectral gap analogue) and discretization bias (via weighted expected squared jump distance) for position-independent irreversible perturbations of ULA.
AI.result: Derives an explicit characterization of the optimal position-independent irreversible perturbation. Numerical experiments show faster convergence with controlled bias and improved mean squared estimation errors.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework enables joint handling of acceleration and accuracy, demonstrating that carefully designed irreversible perturbations can improve Langevin sampling without severely compromising discretization bias.

title: Infinite sequences with optimal diaphony, periodic $L_2$-discrepancy, and beyond
summary: We investigate the periodic $L_2$-discrepancy of infinite sequences $§_d$ in $[0,1)^d$ and its analytic counterpart, the diaphony. We prove that infinite order-2 digital sequences over $\mathbb{F}_2$ attain the optimal order $L_{2,N}^{\rm per}(§_d) \le C_d (\log N)^{d/2}/N$ for all $N \in \mathbb{N}\setminus \{1\}$, matching known lower bounds for infinitely many $N \in \mathbb{N}$. This confirms the conjectured optimality of order-2 constructions. By this result, we improve upon previously known constructions using order-5 digital sequences, and reduce the underlying dimension for the interlacing construction from $5d$ to $2d$, significantly improving practicality. We establish our bounds within a broader framework of quasi-Monte Carlo integration for periodic Besov spaces $S_{p,q}^rB(\mathbb{T}^d)$ with dominating mixed smoothness $r \in (1/p,2)$, where $p,q\in [1,\infty]$. Rules based on infinite order-2 digital sequences yield worst-case errors of order $(\log N)^{(d-1)(1-1/q)} / N^{ \min(r,1)}$ for $r \not=1$, and $(\log N)^{d(1-1/q)}/N$ for $r=1$, for all $N \in \mathbb{N}\setminus\{1\}$, while preserving extensibility in $N$.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that infinite order-2 digital sequences over 𝔽₂ achieve optimal periodic L₂-discrepancy, matching theoretical lower bounds. This improves upon previous order-5 constructions, reducing the interlacing dimension from 5d to 2d, and establishes optimal quasi-Monte Carlo error bounds for periodic Besov spaces with dominating mixed smoothness.
AI.motivation: To confirm the conjectured optimality of order-2 digital sequences for periodic L₂-discrepancy and to improve the practicality of quasi-Monte Carlo methods by reducing the interlacing dimension from 5d to 2d.
AI.method: The authors analyze periodic L₂-discrepancy and diaphony of infinite sequences, proving that order-2 digital sequences over 𝔽₂ achieve optimal convergence rates. They establish bounds within a broader quasi-Monte Carlo integration framework for periodic Besov spaces with dominating mixed smoothness.
AI.result: Order-2 digital sequences achieve optimal periodic L₂-discrepancy L₂,ₙ^(per)(S_d) ≤ C_d (log N)^(d/2)/N for all N, matching lower bounds. Worst-case errors for Besov spaces are of order (log N)^((d-1)(1-1/q))/N^(min(r,1)) for r≠1 and (log N)^(d(1-1/q))/N for r=1.
AI.conclusion: Order-2 digital sequences are optimal for periodic discrepancy, improving over order-5 constructions. The reduced dimension (from 5d to 2d) significantly enhances practicality for quasi-Monte Carlo methods, while preserving extensibility in N.

title: Exact solution of the Gaunt-modified Landau-Lifshitz equation in a plane wave
summary: We analyze electron dynamics in a plane electromagnetic wave using the Landau-Lifshitz equation with a quantum radiation reaction correction modeled by a Gaunt factor. In this geometry, the quantum parameter $χ$ depends solely on the lightfront momentum, allowing the modified equation of motion to retain the integrable structure of the classical problem. We derive an exact solution for the energy evolution and the four-velocity, which reduces to the known classical result in the appropriate limit. The results provide an analytical and deterministic description of semiclassical radiation reaction in plane-wave fields.

AI.tldr: The paper analytically solves electron motion in a plane electromagnetic wave with quantum radiation reaction corrections, obtaining exact solutions for energy and four-velocity that generalize classical results.
AI.motivation: To incorporate quantum radiation reaction effects into electron dynamics in strong electromagnetic fields, beyond the classical Landau-Lifshitz framework, by using a Gaunt factor correction while maintaining analytical solvability.
AI.method: The authors modify the Landau-Lifshitz equation with a quantum correction via a Gaunt factor. Leveraging the fact that the quantum parameter χ depends only on lightfront momentum in plane-wave geometry, the equations retain integrability. Exact analytical solutions for energy and four-velocity are then derived.
AI.result: Exact analytical solutions for electron energy evolution and four-velocity in a plane wave with semiclassical radiation reaction, which reduce to known classical results in the appropriate limit.
AI.conclusion: A deterministic, analytical description of semiclassical radiation reaction in plane-wave fields has been achieved, extending classical theory to include quantum corrections.

title: Finite Element-Based Material Learning via Automatic Differentiation: Learning constitutive neural network models from full-field deformation data
summary: The identification of constitutive neural network models from heterogeneous full-field deformation data provides a robust alternative to traditional calibration methods based on homogeneous stress-strain experiments, particularly given the high dimensionality of trainable parameters. Existing approaches must balance generality, robustness, and computational efficiency: Conventional finite element model updating is broadly applicable but computationally demanding; weak-form methods offer efficiency but are sensitive to noise and data scarcity; neural operator models are highly expressive but require extensive training datasets. This work presents FE-MAD (Finite Element-Based Material learning via Automatic Differentiation), an end-to-end differentiable framework that integrates a constitutive neural network model within a JAX-FEM nonlinear solver and identifies its parameters through gradient-based minimization of a measurement-mismatch loss. Newton tangent stiffness and loss gradients are computed automatically using forward- and reverse-mode automatic differentiation throughout the entire pipeline, thereby removing the need for analytic adjoints or offline surrogate models. FE-MAD is demonstrated for two architectures: a grey-box Constitutive Artificial Neural Network (CANN), a polyconvex, fully connected model with high flexibility, and a white-box CANN, an expert-system network with phenomenologically interpretable strain-energy terms. Focusing on incompressible isotropic hyperelasticity, FE-MAD is evaluated on three open experimental datasets: (1) full digital image correlation (DIC) of a perforated tensile specimen, (2) a reduced-data scenario with a one-dimensional stretch profile and global force-displacement curve, and (3) a heterogeneous matrix-inclusion system in which both phases constitutive laws are identified and generalized to twenty-two previously unseen samples.

AI.tldr: FE-MAD is an end-to-end differentiable framework integrating constitutive neural networks within a JAX-FEM solver for identifying material parameters from heterogeneous full-field deformation data, demonstrating robust performance across multiple experimental datasets without requiring analytic adjoints or offline surrogate models.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for constitutive model identification face trade-offs between generality (FEM updating), efficiency (weak-form methods), and expressiveness (neural operators). The authors aim to develop a framework that balances all three—broad applicability, computational efficiency, and robustness—without manual derivation of adjoints or surrogate training.
AI.method: FE-MAD integrates a constitutive neural network within a JAX-FEM nonlinear solver, using automatic differentiation (forward/reverse mode) to compute Newton tangent stiffness and loss gradients end-to-end. It employs gradient-based minimization of a measurement-mismatch loss. Two architectures are tested: grey-box CANN (flexible polyconvex network) and white-box CANN (interpretable expert-system network).
AI.result: FE-MAD is validated on three datasets: (1) full DIC data of a perforated tensile specimen; (2) a reduced-data scenario with 1D stretch and global force-displacement; (3) a heterogeneous matrix-inclusion system where both phases' laws are identified and generalized to 22 unseen samples. Results demonstrate robust parameter identification under varying data conditions.
AI.conclusion: FE-MAD effectively balances generality, robustness, and efficiency for constitutive model identification from heterogeneous data, removing the need for analytic adjoints or surrogate models. Its differentiability and integration with JAX-FEM make it a promising tool for data-driven material learning.

title: Thin Domains, Reduction, and Slow Manifolds
summary: We propose a unified framework for dimension reduction of partial differential equations posed on thin domains. Our approach combines three complementary ingredients: a careful boundary-condition analysis, an averaging-based splitting for general thin geometries, and a slow-manifold viewpoint for the resulting fast-slow system. Homogeneous Neumann conditions on the thin faces emerge as the most relevant and physical regime because they preserve the transverse zero mode and therefore lead to a genuine lower-dimensional reduced equation. For general thin domains we derive the averaged fast-slow system and isolate the geometry-induced correction term. We then formulate a splitting-based Lyapunov-Perron construction for an exact slow manifold when a suitable spectral decomposition of the slow variable is available, and we construct approximate slow manifolds and corrected reduced dynamics directly from the invariance equation by asymptotic expansion. Moreover, we propose the Schnakenberg reaction-diffusion system as a canonical test problem for comparing the full thin-domain dynamics, the averaged model, and the manifold-corrected reduced dynamics. Finally, we also extend the framework to thin tubular domains and derive the corresponding rescaled fast-slow system in curved geometry.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a unified framework for dimension reduction of PDEs on thin domains by combining boundary-condition analysis, averaging-based splitting, and slow-manifold theory, with applications to Schnakenberg systems and thin tubular geometries.
AI.motivation: To systematically reduce the dimension of PDEs posed on thin domains (e.g., thin layers, tubes) by identifying the most physically relevant boundary conditions and geometric corrections that preserve essential dynamics.
AI.method: Combines three ingredients: (1) boundary-condition analysis identifying homogeneous Neumann conditions as preserving the transverse zero mode; (2) averaging-based splitting to derive fast-slow systems with geometry-induced correction terms; (3) Lyapunov-Perron construction for exact slow manifolds and asymptotic expansions for approximate slow manifolds from the invariance equation.
AI.result: Derives averaged fast-slow systems with geometry-induced corrections; constructs exact and approximate slow manifolds; demonstrates the framework on Schnakenberg reaction-diffusion systems as a canonical test problem; extends the framework to thin tubular domains with curved geometry.
AI.conclusion: Homogeneous Neumann conditions are the most relevant boundary condition for thin-domain reduction; the unified framework provides systematic dimension reduction with geometric corrections, validated on Schnakenberg systems and extended to tubular geometries.

title: Weighted hp-Uniform Decompositions for H^k-Type Tensor-Product Spaces in Arbitrary Dimension
summary: We establish weighted hp-uniform vertex-patch decompositions in arbitrary space dimension d >= 1 for tensor-product discretizations of H^k-type conforming and nonconforming spaces, with arbitrary fixed Sobolev order k >= 1, on fitted interface meshes. The cells are coordinate-compatible cuboids, the local spaces are Q_{p_K}(K) with arbitrary elementwise degrees satisfying p_K >= 2k-1, and the coefficient may have arbitrarily large jumps across material interfaces. Under local coefficient oscillation bounds and a local high-side connectivity condition, both the conforming H^k space and the nonconforming spaces V_h^{(s)}, 0 <= s <= k, admit stable decompositions with constants which may depend on the fixed parameters d and k, but are independent of the mesh size, all polynomial degrees, neighboring degree ratios, and the global coefficient contrast. The argument combines a Hermite endpoint transform for endpoint jets of order 0,...,k-1, its tensor-product extension, weighted broken patch Poincare inequalities, and a successive correction of normal derivative jumps. Numerical experiments for a three-dimensional DG problem with large coefficient jumps and strongly varying local polynomial degrees support the predicted robustness. For k = 1 the same conclusions hold on uniformly regular mapped cubical meshes whose neighboring element maps agree on each common face.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes weighted hp-uniform vertex-patch decompositions for conforming H^k and nonconforming spaces on interface-fitted meshes in arbitrary dimensions, with constants independent of mesh size, polynomial degrees, and coefficient contrast, supported by 3D DG numerical experiments.
AI.motivation: To develop robust decomposition methods for high-order discretizations on interface meshes with large coefficient jumps and varying polynomial degrees, where existing methods lack dimension-independent guarantees.
AI.method: Combines Hermite endpoint transform, tensor-product extension, weighted broken patch Poincaré inequalities, and successive correction of normal derivative jumps to achieve stable vertex-patch decompositions.
AI.result: Constants depend only on fixed parameters d and k, not on mesh size, polynomial degrees, neighboring degree ratios, or global coefficient contrast. Validated by 3D DG experiments with large jumps and varying degrees.
AI.conclusion: The proposed hp-uniform decompositions are robust in high dimensions, high order, and heterogeneous coefficients, with k=1 extending to mapped cubical meshes.

title: Behavior of kinetic instabilities in a dynamically forming resonant distribution
summary: Instabilities driven by energetic particles are central to the physics of a burning plasma. The majority of kinetic simulations and reduced models assume that the unstable distribution is already fully established when energetic-particle-driven modes grow unstable. In realistic scenarios, however, energetic particles may accumulate in the resonance on an effective timescale comparable to the growth rate of the instability, meaning that the formation of the resonant distribution and the growth of the unstable mode must be treated concurrently. We study the behavior of these instabilities in the presence of such a dynamically forming distribution, evaluating two distinct metrics which measure how close a mode is to its linear stability threshold and how close a mode remains to its nonlinear stability threshold. It is found that saturation at large $ω_b/ν_\text{eff}$ (where $ω_b$ is the bounce frequency of deeply trapped particles and $ν_\text{eff}$ is the effective scattering rate at a resonance), normally associated with strongly driven excitation, can be achieved even if dynamically the mode remains at all times near its nonlinear stability threshold. We extend existing analytic models for near-marginal and far from marginal modes allowing for a time-dependent linear growth rate, deriving explicit expressions for the mode amplitude evolution. These formulas are shown to agree with nonlinear kinetic simulations. The discrepancies between the case of a dynamically forming distribution and the case of a fully formed distribution are shown to be particularly pronounced for energetic particle distributions which relax diffusively.

AI.tldr: This paper studies energetic-particle-driven instabilities in burning plasmas, focusing on scenarios where the resonant particle distribution forms concurrently with mode growth. It finds that saturation at large ω_b/ν_eff can occur even when the mode remains near its nonlinear stability threshold, and extends analytic models for time-dependent linear growth rates, validated with simulations. Discrepancies are most significant for diffusively relaxing distributions.
AI.motivation: To understand instabilities driven by energetic particles in burning plasmas where the resonant distribution forms on timescales comparable to mode growth, requiring concurrent treatment of distribution formation and instability growth.
AI.method: Evaluates two metrics measuring proximity to linear and nonlinear stability thresholds; extends analytic models for near-marginal and far-from-marginal modes with time-dependent linear growth rates; validates with nonlinear kinetic simulations.
AI.result: Saturation at large ω_b/ν_eff can be achieved even when the mode remains near its nonlinear stability threshold. Derived expressions for mode amplitude evolution agree with simulations. Discrepancies between dynamically forming and fully formed distributions are pronounced for diffusively relaxing distributions.
AI.conclusion: Existing models are extended to account for concurrent distribution formation and mode growth, with significant implications for predicting instability behavior in burning plasmas, especially for diffusive relaxation cases.

title: A differentiable machine learning small-angle X-ray scattering analysis framework for structure elucidation of lipid nanoparticles
summary: Lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) are efficient delivery systems for negatively charged nucleic acids. Their multi-component architecture yields a core-shell structure. Small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) is an important characterization technique for LNPs, but recovering internal structure and size distribution from SAXS is an inverse problem with non-unique solutions. Realistic models are often too expensive for systematic exploration. We introduce a machine-learning-accelerated, differentiable framework for SAXS analysis of heterogeneous, polydisperse LNPs. The forward model combines a core-shell particle with a Gaussian random-field interior, a neural surrogate for the monodisperse SAXS map, and a differentiable layer integrating over particle-size distributions. The surrogate reduces prediction cost by four orders of magnitude, while differentiability enables large-scale multi-start fitting and ensemble identifiability analysis. Applied to synthetic and experimental MC3 LNP data, the framework shows that near-identical SAXS fits can arise from distinct parameter modes, with the experimental fits dominated by a trade-off between size-distribution and interior-structure parameters.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a machine-learning-accelerated differentiable framework for SAXS analysis of LNPs, using a neural surrogate to reduce prediction cost by 10,000x and enabling large-scale fitting, revealing that near-identical SAXS fits can arise from distinct parameter modes with trade-offs between size-distribution and interior-structure parameters.
AI.motivation: Lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) are important for nucleic acid delivery, but analyzing their internal structure from SAXS data is an inverse problem with non-unique solutions. Realistic models are computationally too expensive for systematic exploration.
AI.method: The framework combines a core-shell particle model with a Gaussian random-field interior, a neural surrogate for the monodisperse SAXS map, and a differentiable layer for integrating over particle-size distributions. The surrogate reduces prediction cost by four orders of magnitude.
AI.result: The framework shows that near-identical SAXS fits can arise from distinct parameter modes. Experimental fits are dominated by a trade-off between size-distribution and interior-structure parameters. Applied to synthetic and experimental MC3 LNP data.
AI.conclusion: Differentiability enables large-scale multi-start fitting and ensemble identifiability analysis, addressing the non-uniqueness challenge in SAXS analysis of LNPs.

title: Robust interpolation inequalities via Chebyshev-type integral inequalities
summary: We establish robust log-convex interpolation inequalities within the scale of Gagliardo seminorms. We achieve this by deriving some Chebyshev-type integral inequalities for general non-synchronous functions. Our primary motivation for establishing these robust interpolation inequalities stems from the study of the asymptotic nonlocal-to-local stability of weak solutions to the boundary Dirichlet problem associated with the regional fractional $p$-Laplacian. More precisely, if $u_s \in W^{s,p}(Ω)$ weakly satisfies $(-Δ)_{p, Ω}^s u_s = f_s $ in $Ω$ and $ γ^s_0(u_s) = g_s$ on $\partialΩ,$ with $\frac{1}{p} < s \leq 1$ and $Ω\subset \mathbb{R}^d$ is bounded Lipschitz, then, under appropriate convergence of the data $f_s$ and $g_s$ as $s \to 1^-$, we establish that $\| u_s - u_1 \|_{W^{η,p}(Ω)} \xrightarrow{s \to 1^-} 0 $ for all $0 \leq η< 1$.

AI.tldr: This paper develops robust log-convex interpolation inequalities in Gagliardo seminorm scales using Chebyshev-type integral inequalities, and applies them to prove strong convergence of weak solutions to the regional fractional p-Laplacian Dirichlet problem as s→1⁻.
AI.motivation: To study the asymptotic nonlocal-to-local stability of weak solutions to the boundary Dirichlet problem associated with the regional fractional p-Laplacian, specifically proving convergence of solutions as the fractional parameter s approaches 1.
AI.method: Derives Chebyshev-type integral inequalities for non-synchronous functions to establish robust log-convex interpolation inequalities within the scale of Gagliardo seminorms.
AI.result: Under appropriate convergence of data f_s and g_s as s→1⁻, the solutions converge: ||u_s - u_1||_{W^{η,p}(Ω)} → 0 for all 0 ≤ η < 1, where u_s ∈ W^{s,p}(Ω) satisfies (-Δ)_{p,Ω}^s u_s = f_s in Ω and γ^s_0(u_s) = g_s on ∂Ω.
AI.conclusion: The robust interpolation inequalities enable proving strong convergence of fractional p-Laplacian solutions to the local limit, establishing nonlocal-to-local stability.

title: A Low-rank Interpolatory Projection Algorithm for Solving Large-scale T-Sylvester Equations
summary: This paper considers large-scale T-Sylvester equations of the form $AX - X^\top E^\top + B_1B_2^\top = 0$, which admit a low-rank solution. It is shown that when the unique solution of the T-Sylvester equation is low-rank, the problem naturally reduces to a tangential interpolation problem via oblique projection. The specific interpolation points and tangential directions needed to obtain the low-rank solution are not known a priori, thus requiring an iterative approach. An iterative interpolatory projection algorithm is proposed based on these interpolation conditions, which iteratively refines the interpolation data as the projection matrices expand in the number of columns. Numerical examples demonstrate that the proposed algorithm converges with projection matrices having significantly fewer columns compared to existing Krylov-subspace-based projection methods, confirming the superiority of the proposed algorithm over existing approaches.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an iterative interpolation-based projection algorithm for solving low-rank T-Sylvester equations, showing it converges faster and requires fewer projection columns than existing Krylov-subspace methods.
AI.motivation: To efficiently solve large-scale T-Sylvester equations that admit low-rank solutions, as existing Krylov-subspace methods require large projection matrices.
AI.method: The problem is reduced to a tangential interpolation problem via oblique projection. An iterative interpolatory projection algorithm is developed that refines interpolation data as projection matrices expand column-wise.
AI.result: The proposed algorithm converges with significantly fewer columns in the projection matrices compared to Krylov-subspace-based methods.
AI.conclusion: The iterative interpolation-based algorithm is superior to existing Krylov-subspace approaches for solving low-rank T-Sylvester equations.

title: Implementation and Verification of Toroidal Resistive Wall Boundary Conditions in the PIXIE3D MHD code using a Boundary Integral Method
summary: We present the complete formulation of resistive wall boundary conditions in axisymmetric toroidal geometries as implemented in the PIXIE3D extended magnetohydrodynamics (MHD) code, along with a complete suite of analytical verification examples that demonstrate correctness in the implementation. The formulation centers around a thin wall approximation and a Boundary Integral Method to solve for the magnetic scalar potential in the immediately surrounding vacuum. This requires specialized quadrature rules derived from existing literature to handle the numerical integration of singular and hypersingular integrands (the Green's function of Laplace's equation and its derivatives), for which we provide the nodes and weights. Further, we describe an extension to the formalism to include the effect of a perfectly conducting second, outer wall exterior to the resistive (plasma-facing) wall and separated by vacuum, and exterior to the computational plasma mesh proper. Lastly, we describe an extension to include the effect of current-carrying coils also defined exterior to the plasma mesh in the resistive wall boundary condition treatment. For most aspects of the method, we present self-contained verification examples using analytic solutions in axisymmetric toroidal geometries (with both 2D and 3D plasmas) and show it to be accurate to second order. We demonstrate the algorithm with a vertical displacement event (VDE) using the ITER tokamak geometry.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a complete formulation of resistive wall boundary conditions in the PIXIE3D MHD code, including thin wall approximation, Boundary Integral Method, specialized quadrature rules, extensions for outer conducting walls and current-carrying coils, verified with analytic examples and demonstrated on an ITER VDE simulation.
AI.motivation: To accurately model resistive wall boundary conditions in toroidal fusion devices, which are critical for plasma stability (e.g., vertical displacement events), and to provide rigorous verification of the numerical implementation.
AI.method: Thin wall approximation + Boundary Integral Method for magnetic scalar potential in vacuum; specialized quadrature rules for singular/hypersingular Green's function integrals; extensions for outer conducting wall and external coils; implemented in PIXIE3D extended MHD code.
AI.result: Self-contained verification with analytic solutions in axisymmetric toroidal geometries (2D and 3D) shows second-order accuracy; demonstrated via ITER VDE simulation.
AI.conclusion: The resistive wall boundary condition treatment is correct, accurate to second order, and applicable to realistic fusion devices like ITER.

title: Multi-Fidelity Learning with Shallow Recurrent Decoders for Reactor Physics
summary: In reactor physics, neutronics can be treated with different fidelity levels, according to the needs of the user. On one hand, the precise modeling of neutrons' behaviour in reactor physics is often expensive and time-consuming due to the high computational costs to numerically solve the Boltzmann transport equation. Conversely, by adopting suitable assumptions, such as the SP$_N$, diffusion theory, and point kinetics, it is possible to generate efficiently low-fidelity data. From the perspective of surrogate models, this computational limitation translates into a scarcity of high-fidelity data and a significant amount of low-fidelity data. Given this difference in fidelity levels, it would be interesting to develop a suitable procedure to map low-fidelity models towards higher fidelity models; for instance, one could obtain the solution to a multi-group diffusion equation starting from time-series data obtained from a point kinetics model. Indeed, this work investigates this possibility by leveraging multi-fidelity information with Shallow Recurrent Decoders, a novel machine learning architecture able to map time-series observations to the full state of the reactor. This technique has been designed to use local or global measurements as input and map their temporal trajectories to the high-dimensional state; by the same logic, in principle, this architecture can also be used when the input is formed by the solution of a lumped model. This work applies this idea to a benchmark reactor geometry, mapping the point kinetics model to the diffusion solution under various input conditions, with much less computational costs.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using Shallow Recurrent Decoders to map low-fidelity point kinetics model outputs to high-fidelity diffusion equation solutions in reactor physics, enabling efficient multi-fidelity modeling with reduced computational costs.
AI.motivation: High-fidelity neutronics modeling (Boltzmann transport equation) is computationally expensive, resulting in scarce high-fidelity data. Meanwhile, low-fidelity models (SP$_N$, diffusion, point kinetics) generate abundant data cheaply. The authors aim to bridge this fidelity gap by mapping low-fidelity to high-fidelity solutions efficiently.
AI.method: The authors employ Shallow Recurrent Decoders, a machine learning architecture that maps time-series observations (from low-fidelity point kinetics models) to the full high-dimensional reactor state (multi-group diffusion solution). The architecture is designed to handle both local and global measurements as input.
AI.result: The method successfully maps point kinetics model outputs to diffusion solutions on a benchmark reactor geometry under various input conditions, achieving high-fidelity results at much lower computational cost.
AI.conclusion: Multi-fidelity information can be effectively leveraged via Shallow Recurrent Decoders to replace expensive high-fidelity simulations with cheap low-fidelity model outputs mapped to high-fidelity solutions, offering a practical surrogate modeling approach for reactor physics.

title: Solutions to critical equations with a superposition of nonlocal Hartree-type nonlinearities
summary: We study a class of nonlinear nonlocal elliptic equations in $\mathbb{R}^N$ involving superpositions of Hartree-type nonlinearities. Motivated by the Schrödinger-Poisson-Slater system, these equations arise as natural generalizations of problems with a single nonlocal interaction term. More precisely, we consider equations driven by a family of Riesz potentials weighted by a positive Borel measure, which gives rise to a superposed nonlocal operator. To treat this problem variationally, we introduce suitable functional settings, namely the superposed Coulomb space and the associated superposed Coulomb-Sobolev space, and study their main properties. Combining variational methods with a recently developed scaling-based critical point theory, we prove existence and multiplicity results for radial solutions. We also investigate a Brezis-Nirenberg-type problem and obtain multiplicity results near eigenvalues of an associated nonlinear eigenvalue problem. Our results extend previous works on single Hartree-type equations and provide a unified framework for treating superpositions of nonlocal interactions of Hartree type.

AI.tldr: This paper studies nonlinear nonlocal elliptic equations with superposed Hartree-type nonlinearities, introducing new functional spaces (superposed Coulomb and Coulomb-Sobolev spaces), and proves existence and multiplicity of radial solutions using variational methods and scaling-based critical point theory.
AI.motivation: Motivated by the Schrödinger-Poisson-Slater system, the authors aim to generalize problems with a single nonlocal interaction term to superpositions of Hartree-type nonlinearities driven by weighted Riesz potentials, providing a unified framework.
AI.method: The authors introduce superposed Coulomb and Coulomb-Sobolev spaces, combine variational methods with scaling-based critical point theory, and also investigate a Brezis-Nirenberg-type problem with analysis near eigenvalues.
AI.result: Existence and multiplicity results for radial solutions are proven; multiplicity results near eigenvalues of an associated nonlinear eigenvalue problem are obtained.
AI.conclusion: The work extends previous results on single Hartree-type equations and establishes a unified framework for handling superpositions of nonlocal Hartree-type interactions.

title: Iterative Thresholding Pursuit with Continuation for $\ell_{1-2}$-Regularized Sparse Recovery
summary: Sparse recovery aims to reconstruct sparse signals from underdetermined and possibly noisy linear measurements. Existing $\ell_{1-2}$ iterative thresholding schemes are first-order methods. We propose an iterative thresholding pursuit method with continuation (ITP-C) for $\ell_{1-2}$-regularized sparse recovery. The method goes beyond first-order thresholding by combining the active-set identification capability of the $\ell_{1-2}$ proximal step with a restricted least-squares pursuit step that provides a second-order update on the identified support. The support is generated adaptively by the thresholding update, and no prior knowledge of the true sparsity level is required. To control the possible instability of the pursuit step while preserving the descent structure of the continuation scheme, we impose a strict descent check with respect to the dynamic objective. We establish convergence of the generated sequence under the Kurdyka-Lojasiewicz framework and prove a local oracle-type property after correct support identification. Numerical experiments on synthetic sparse recovery and image reconstruction illustrate the descent preservation of the proposed safeguard and demonstrate the improved recovery performance of ITP-C over the state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes ITP-C, a novel iterative thresholding pursuit method with continuation for ℓ₁₋₂-regularized sparse recovery that combines first-order thresholding (for active-set identification) with a second-order pursuit step (for refined updates on the identified support), incorporates a strict descent check for stability, and demonstrates improved recovery performance over state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing ℓ₁₋₂ iterative thresholding schemes are limited to first-order methods and may not fully exploit the structure of the solution. The authors aim to go beyond first-order thresholding to improve recovery accuracy and efficiency.
AI.method: ITP-C combines (1) an ℓ₁₋₂ proximal step for active-set identification (first-order thresholding) with (2) a restricted least-squares pursuit step providing second-order updates on the identified support. A strict descent check w.r.t. the dynamic objective controls instability, and a continuation scheme preserves descent structure. No prior sparsity level knowledge is required.
AI.result: The method achieves descent preservation via the proposed safeguard. Numerical experiments on synthetic sparse recovery and image reconstruction show ITP-C outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in recovery performance.
AI.conclusion: Convergence is established under the Kurdyka-Lojasiewicz framework, and a local oracle-type property after correct support identification is proven. ITP-C effectively bridges first-order thresholding and second-order pursuit for ℓ₁₋₂ sparse recovery.

title: Coupled simulation of plasma-surface interactions during early stages of vacuum arcing
summary: We describe fully coupled simulations that bridge atomistic cathode dynamics and plasma formation during the earliest stages of vacuum arcing. The model combines molecular dynamics, finite element electrothermal calculations, electron emission and particle-in-cell plasma simulations via dynamic transfer of particles between the surface and plasma domains. Simulations of Cu nanoprotrusions reveal two routes to thermal runaway: direct Joule heating-driven instability and a novel nanoparticle-assisted mechanism, where detached nanoparticles generate neutral vapor that becomes ionized.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a multiscale simulation framework that couples atomistic cathode dynamics with plasma formation during vacuum arc initiation, revealing two thermal runaway mechanisms in Cu nanoprotrusions: direct Joule heating and a nanoparticle-assisted vapor ionization process.
AI.motivation: To develop a fully coupled simulation model that bridges the gap between atomistic cathode dynamics and plasma formation during the earliest stages of vacuum arcing, which has not been previously achieved.
AI.method: A hybrid model combining molecular dynamics, finite element electrothermal calculations, electron emission, and particle-in-cell plasma simulations with dynamic particle transfer between surface and plasma domains.
AI.result: Two routes to thermal runaway in Cu nanoprotrusions are identified: (1) direct Joule heating-driven instability, and (2) a novel nanoparticle-assisted mechanism where detached nanoparticles generate neutral vapor that becomes ionized.
AI.conclusion: The nanoparticle-assisted thermal runaway represents a previously unknown mechanism for vacuum arc initiation, suggesting that nanoparticle detachment and subsequent vapor ionization plays a critical role in arc formation.

title: Newton's Identity in Finite-Bead Fermionic Partition Function
summary: For non-interacting fermions in a harmonic trap, the partition function at any discrete number of imaginary time slices (or beads) and for any choice of short-time propagator admits an exact recursion relation derived directly from the contracted determinant form of the path integral. This finite-bead recursion is distinct from earlier continuum-limit recursions, which do not apply to the discrete time partition functions. By identifying a direct correspondence between this recursion and Newton's identity, application of a closed-form result from the theory of partitions provides an exact expression for the one-dimensional $n$-fermion finite-bead partition function. From this, the Thermodynamic and Hamiltonian energies and specific heats are analytically calculated for any $n$, $N$, $τ$, and propagator choice.

AI.tldr: This paper derives an exact recursion relation for non-interacting fermions in a harmonic trap using path integrals with finite imaginary time slices, connects it to Newton's identity, and obtains closed-form partition functions for any finite bead number, enabling analytical calculation of thermodynamic quantities.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of exact recursion relations for discrete-time (finite-bead) path integral partition functions for fermions in harmonic traps, as opposed to existing continuum-limit recursions which do not apply.
AI.method: Derives a finite-bead recursion from the contracted determinant form of the path integral, links it to Newton's identity, and applies a closed-form result from partition theory to obtain exact expressions for the n-fermion partition function.
AI.result: An exact closed-form expression for the one-dimensional n-fermion finite-bead partition function is obtained, from which Thermodynamic and Hamiltonian energies and specific heats are analytically calculated for any number of particles, beads, imaginary time, and propagator choice.
AI.conclusion: The discrete-time recursion provides a powerful exact framework for computing thermodynamic observables of non-interacting fermions in harmonic traps, generalizable across bead numbers and propagator choices.

title: Interface problems of mixed spatial order
summary: We solve interface problems on the line between various constant coefficient linear evolution partial differential equations. Our prototypical examples are the heat, linear Schrödinger, Airy, linearized Korteweg de Vries, and biharmonic Schrödinger equations. In each problem, one of the listed equations is posed on one spatial half line, and another on the other half line, with appropriate interface conditions. These problems are solved by means of novel extensions of Fokas's unified transform method and the explicit solution formulae are evaluated using Filon quadrature.

AI.tldr: This paper develops novel extensions of Fokas's unified transform method to solve interface problems on the line between different constant coefficient linear evolution PDEs (heat, Schrödinger, Airy, linearized KdV, biharmonic Schrödinger) with interface conditions, and evaluates the explicit solution formulas using Filon quadrature.
AI.motivation: To solve interface problems on the line where different linear evolution PDEs are posed on each half-line with interface conditions, using extensions of Fokas's unified transform method.
AI.method: Novel extensions of Fokas's unified transform method for interface problems, with explicit solution formulae evaluated using Filon quadrature.
AI.result: Explicit solution formulae for interface problems between various constant coefficient linear evolution PDEs on the line, evaluated via Filon quadrature.
AI.conclusion: The Fokas unified transform method can be extended to solve interface problems between different linear evolution PDEs on the line, with explicit solutions computable via Filon quadrature.

title: Auxiliary Gradient-Flow Solvers for Generalized Newtonian Models
summary:
We introduce an auxiliary gradient-flow framework for variational problems with generalized Newtonian structure governed by an N-function. The key idea is to replace the nonlinear constitutive dependence on the gradient, or symmetric gradient, by an auxiliary scalar variable representing its squared magnitude. This shifts the nonlinearity from the state equation to the auxiliary variable, yielding a sequence of uniformly elliptic weighted linear problems.
  At the continuous level, we construct an auxiliary energy on a metric space adapted to the growth of the underlying N-function. In this topology, we prove lower semicontinuity, geodesic $λ$-convexity, and exponential convergence of the associated minimizing-movement scheme. At the finite element level, we derive a metric gradient flow through an explicit Riesz map, prove global well-posedness of the resulting semi-discrete ODE, and establish convergence to the finite element solution of the Euler--Lagrange equations of the generalized Newtonian energy. For the $p$-Laplacian and $p$-Stokes models, this gives a rigorous convergence result for $4/3\le p\le 4$, $p\ne2$, with asymptotic rate estimates beyond this range.
  We also propose practical time discretizations, including an operator-splitting scheme that gives the \kac iteration as a special case, and an adaptive pseudo-transient method that can be implemented using scalable linear solvers. Numerical experiments for power-law, Carreau--Yasuda, regularized Bingham, and optimal-design models demonstrate robustness, mesh-independent iteration counts in the tested regimes, and performance that matches or outperforms Newton's method.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an auxiliary gradient-flow framework for generalized Newtonian variational problems, shifting nonlinearity to an auxiliary scalar variable. It proves theoretical convergence (for p-Laplacian/Stokes with 4/3≤p≤4), proposes practical time discretizations including operator-splitting (kac iteration) and adaptive pseudo-transient methods, and demonstrates robust numerical performance across multiple models.
AI.motivation: To develop a robust and efficient numerical framework for generalized Newtonian problems (p-Laplacian, p-Stokes, Carreau-Yasuda, Bingham, optimal-design) that handles nonlinear gradient dependence by shifting it to an auxiliary scalar variable, enabling uniformly elliptic weighted linear problems and provable convergence.
AI.method: An auxiliary gradient-flow framework where the nonlinear constitutive dependence on the (symmetric) gradient is replaced by an auxiliary scalar variable representing its squared magnitude. This yields a sequence of uniformly elliptic weighted linear problems. At the continuous level: auxiliary energy on a metric space with lower semicontinuity, geodesic λ-convexity, and minimizing-movement scheme. At the finite element level: metric gradient flow via explicit Riesz map, semi-discrete ODE, and convergence to Euler-Lagrange solutions. Practical discretizations include operator-splitting (kac iteration) and adaptive pseudo-transient methods.
AI.result: Rigorous convergence results for p-Laplacian and p-Stokes for 4/3≤p≤4 (p≠2) with asymptotic rate estimates beyond this range. Numerical experiments for power-law, Carreau-Yasuda, regularized Bingham, and optimal-design models show robust, mesh-independent iteration counts and performance matching or outperforming Newton's method.
AI.conclusion: The proposed auxiliary gradient-flow framework provides a theoretically grounded and numerically efficient approach for a broad class of generalized Newtonian variational problems, with provable convergence guarantees and practical advantages over traditional Newton solvers.

title: Optimization of EUV output by experimentally validated radiation-hydrodynamic simulations across a broad laser parameter space
comment: The following article has been submitted to/accepted by Applied Physics Letters. After it is published, it will be found at https://pubs.aip.org/aip/apl
summary: Practical requirements such as improving wall-plug efficiency and reducing system footprint have become increasingly important with the introduction of extreme ultraviolet (EUV) lithography into high-volume semiconductor manufacturing. These demands motivate the development of solid-state mid-infrared lasers as alternatives to current CO2 lasers. Systematic exploration of laser-to-EUV conversion efficiency (EUV-CE) over a broad parameter space is essential when altering the drive laser's wavelength, because the EUV-CE depends on the laser parameters in a complex manner. In this work, we performed a large-scale grid search of more than 140,000 parameter combinations for laser-produced tin plasma EUV sources using the radiation-hydrodynamics code STAR-1D, which is validated against EUV source experiments. The systematic wavelength dependence of the optimum pulse width and target size is governed by the requirement to simultaneously achieve the electron temperature and density optimal for EUV emission, maintain efficient laser absorption, and suppress EUV self-absorption. The resulting CE map predicts a global maximum of 5.63% at 5.5 μm. For the practically relevant 2 μm solid-state driver, a maximum CE of 4.64% is obtained, in good agreement with recent experimental results. Multiple operating points are identified over a broad range of pulse parameters, providing guidance for 2 μm-driven EUV source development.

AI.tldr: This paper uses a large-scale simulation search (>140,000 parameter combinations) with the STAR-1D code to systematically map EUV conversion efficiency for laser-produced tin plasma sources across different drive laser wavelengths, finding a global maximum of 5.63% at 5.5 μm and 4.64% for 2 μm solid-state drivers, providing guidance for developing alternatives to CO2 lasers in EUV lithography.
AI.motivation: Practical demands for improving wall-plug efficiency and reducing system footprint in high-volume EUV lithography motivate developing solid-state mid-infrared lasers as alternatives to current CO2 lasers, requiring systematic understanding of laser-to-EUV conversion efficiency across broad parameter spaces.
AI.method: A large-scale grid search of over 140,000 parameter combinations was performed using the radiation-hydrodynamics code STAR-1D (validated against experimental data) to simulate laser-produced tin plasma EUV sources, systematically exploring wavelength dependence of optimum pulse width and target size.
AI.result: Global maximum EUV conversion efficiency of 5.63% predicted at 5.5 μm; for 2 μm solid-state drivers, a maximum CE of 4.64% was obtained (matching recent experiments); multiple operating points identified across broad pulse parameter ranges.
AI.conclusion: The wavelength-dependent optimal pulse parameters are governed by the trade-off between achieving optimal electron temperature/density for EUV emission, maintaining efficient laser absorption, and suppressing EUV self-absorption. Results provide guidance for developing 2 μm-driven EUV sources.

title: Towards stable and accurate electron dynamics via neural network based time-dependent variational Monte Carlo
summary: Real-time dynamics of interacting electrons lies at the interface between quantum mechanics and non-equilibrium physics, governing the microscopic origin of ultrafast phenomena of molecules and nano-materials. Though neural network variational Monte Carlo has achieved unprecedented accuracy for stationary state calculations, its extension to real-time evolution remains challenging. In this work, we introduce the neural basis time-dependent variational Monte Carlo framework, which achieves stable and highly accurate simulations of electron dynamics. By constraining the time evolution to a compact, customized manifold spanned by the neural basis, we effectively bypass instability issues and achieve long-term stable evolution. Moreover, we demonstrate that this framework yields benchmark-quality accuracy in simulating the laser-driven dipole responses of the hydrogen atom and a stretched hydrogen molecule, and accurately extracts the dynamic polarizabilities of helium and beryllium atoms. Our work reveals the vast potential of neural network wavefunctions for accurately describing real-time electron dynamics and establishes a promising new route for first-principles simulations of complex, time-dependent electronic phenomena.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a neural basis time-dependent variational Monte Carlo framework that enables stable, long-term, and highly accurate simulations of real-time electron dynamics, demonstrated on atoms and molecules.
AI.motivation: Real-time dynamics of interacting electrons is crucial for understanding ultrafast phenomena, but neural network variational Monte Carlo methods, despite their success in stationary states, struggle with real-time evolution due to instability issues.
AI.method: The authors propose a neural basis time-dependent variational Monte Carlo (tb-VMC) framework that constrains time evolution to a compact, customized manifold spanned by the neural basis, effectively bypassing instability and enabling long-term stable evolution.
AI.result: The framework achieves benchmark-quality accuracy in simulating laser-driven dipole responses of hydrogen atom and stretched hydrogen molecule, and accurately extracts dynamic polarizabilities of helium and beryllium atoms.
AI.conclusion: This work reveals the vast potential of neural network wavefunctions for real-time electron dynamics and establishes a promising new route for first-principles simulations of complex time-dependent electronic phenomena.

title: Sharp mass-threshold for Dancer-type solutions of the focusing mass-critical NLS on $\Bbb R^d\times\Bbb T$
summary: The mass-critical NLS on Euclidean space $\R^d$ exhibits a strong mass rigidity: all positive ground states are generated from a single profile and have the same ground state mass $\widehat{M}(Q)$. By appealing to bifurcation methods, Dancer constructed in his seminar paper \cite{DancerSolution} solutions to the corresponding equation on $\R^d\times\T$ which decay in the noncompact directions and are nontrivially periodic in one direction. Such bifurcation approach, however, does not provide any energetic characterization of the solutions, and in particular does not explain their relation to the Euclidean ground-states. By introducing a new strict monotonicity mechanism for the prescribed-mass energy level, combining the semivirial-vanishing geometry framework developed in author's recent work, we prove that for any mass $c\in(0,2π\widehat{M}(Q))$ the semivirial-vanishing variational problem $m_c$ admits a normalized Dancer-type optimizer which also solves the focusing mass-critical NLS on $\R^d\times\T$. This also gives a sharp complement for the existence results deduced in our earlier work \cite{Luo_LegendreFenchel} via the Legendre-Fenchel duality.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes a variational characterization of Dancer-type solutions to the mass-critical NLS on \(\mathbb{R}^d \times \mathbb{T}\), proving they arise as optimizers of a semivirial-vanishing variational problem for masses below a threshold, thereby bridging bifurcation constructions with energetic properties.
AI.motivation: To provide an energetic/ variational characterization of Dancer-type solutions to the mass-critical NLS on product spaces, which were previously only known via bifurcation methods without explaining their relation to Euclidean ground states.
AI.method: Introduces a strict monotonicity mechanism for the prescribed-mass energy level and combines it with a semivirial-vanishing geometry framework (from the author's prior work) to define and solve a variational problem whose optimizers yield normalized Dancer-type solutions.
AI.result: For any mass \(c \in (0, 2\pi \widehat{M}(Q))\), the semivirial-vanishing variational problem \(m_c\) admits a normalized Dancer-type optimizer that solves the focusing mass-critical NLS on \(\mathbb{R}^d \times \mathbb{T}\). This provides a sharp complement to earlier existence results via Legendre-Fenchel duality.
AI.conclusion: The work bridges the gap between bifurcation existence results and variational/energetic characterizations for Dancer-type solutions on noncompact product spaces, revealing their relation to Euclidean ground states and completing the existence theory.

title: Direct reconstruction for acoustic inverse Born scattering
summary: We consider the inverse medium scattering problem for the Helmholtz equation in two dimensions, i.e., the task to recover a compactly supported penetrable two-dimensional scatterer from full knowledge of the associated far field data or, equivalently, the far field operator. Although this problem is uniquely solvable, it is severely ill-posed and nonlinear. In the regime of weak scattering, the Born approximation yields a linearized relation between the contrast and the far field data, thus overcoming the second difficulty. This linear setting allows to build on recent work on linearized electrical impedance tomography, which relies on triangular Zernike decompositions, to derive an explicit reconstruction formula that expresses the expansion coefficients of the contrast in terms of those of the far field data. By choosing the expansion functions appropriately, the resulting system matrix decouples into separate (infinite) triangular systems for the spatial angular frequencies in the contrast. Consequently, each of these systems can be solved independently by performing forward substitutions. Our numerical experiments indicate that this approach, combined with an adequate regularization method, remains effective even when applied to full nonlinear far field data beyond the Born regime.

AI.tldr: The paper develops an explicit reconstruction formula for the inverse medium scattering problem using triangular Zernike decompositions, which decouples the system into independent triangular subsystems solvable by forward substitution, and shows effectiveness even beyond the Born approximation regime.
AI.motivation: To solve the inverse medium scattering problem for the Helmholtz equation, which is severely ill-posed and nonlinear, by finding a method that overcomes nonlinearity while remaining effective beyond weak scattering regimes.
AI.method: The method uses the Born approximation to linearize the relation between contrast and far field data, then applies triangular Zernike decompositions (building on work from linearized EIT) to derive an explicit reconstruction formula where expansion coefficients of the contrast are expressed in terms of far field data coefficients. The system matrix decouples into independent triangular subsystems for each angular frequency, solvable via forward substitution.
AI.result: Numerical experiments demonstrate that the approach, combined with adequate regularization, remains effective even when applied to full nonlinear far field data beyond the Born regime.
AI.conclusion: The triangular Zernike decomposition approach provides an explicit, computationally efficient reconstruction method for inverse medium scattering that works beyond the linearized Born approximation when paired with proper regularization.

title: Wave drag in moving plasmas: recent developments and prospects
summary: Wave propagation in a medium differs depending on whether this medium is at rest or moving with respect to an observer. Motion can notably lead to modifications of the wave trajectory, of its polarization, or of its transverse structure. Although these effects are well documented in isotropic dielectrics, they remain largely unexplored and unaccounted for in plasmas, despite the fact that simple models suggest they could in fact be large under certain conditions, as well as recent experimental observations. Here we first review existing models for motion effects on plasma waves, then identify a number of basic challenges that lie in the way of using these models to quantify motion effects in realistic configurations, and finally discuss possible workarounds.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews how wave propagation is affected when a plasma medium moves relative to an observer, identifies gaps in existing models for realistic plasma configurations, and proposes workarounds.
AI.motivation: Motion effects on wave propagation are well-studied in isotropic dielectrics but remain largely unexplored in plasmas, despite simple models and recent experiments suggesting these effects could be significant under certain conditions.
AI.method: The authors first review existing models for motion effects on plasma waves, then identify basic challenges in applying these models to realistic configurations, and finally discuss possible workarounds.
AI.result: The paper identifies that existing models are insufficient for quantifying motion effects in realistic plasma configurations, and there are several basic challenges that must be addressed.
AI.conclusion: Simple models and recent experiments suggest motion effects in plasmas could be large, but current models face significant challenges in realistic settings, necessitating further development and workarounds.

title: Schrodinger dynamics and Berry phase of undulatory locomotion
comment: title change; additional section and figure on time-dependent effective Hamiltonians and Berry phases; additional biological examples; new numerical analysis added to the SI
summary: Spectral mode representations play an essential role in various areas of physics, from quantum mechanics to fluid turbulence, but they are not yet extensively used to characterize and describe the behavioral dynamics of living systems. Here, we show that mode-based linear models inferred from experimental live-imaging data can provide an accurate low-dimensional description of undulatory locomotion in worms, centipedes, robots, and snakes. By incorporating physical symmetries and known biological constraints into the dynamical model, we find that the shape dynamics are generically governed by Schrodinger equations in mode space. The eigenstates of the effective biophysical Hamiltonians and their adiabatic variations enable the efficient classification and differentiation of locomotion behaviors in natural, simulated, and robotic organisms using Grassmann distances and Berry phases. While our analysis focuses on a widely studied class of biophysical locomotion phenomena, the underlying approach generalizes to other physical or living systems that permit a mode representation subject to geometric shape constraints.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that mode-based linear models from experimental imaging data can provide low-dimensional descriptions of undulatory locomotion in worms, centipedes, robots, and snakes. Shape dynamics are governed by Schrödinger equations in mode space, and eigenstates enable classification of locomotion behaviors using Grassmann distances and Berry phases.
AI.motivation: Spectral mode representations are widely used in physics (quantum mechanics, fluid turbulence) but are not yet extensively applied to characterize behavioral dynamics of living systems. The authors aim to bridge this gap.
AI.method: Mode-based linear models are inferred from experimental live-imaging data. Physical symmetries and biological constraints are incorporated into the dynamical model, yielding Schrödinger-like equations in mode space. Grassmann distances and Berry phases are used for classification.
AI.result: Shape dynamics are generically governed by Schrödinger equations in mode space. Eigenstates of effective biophysical Hamiltonians and their adiabatic variations enable efficient classification and differentiation of locomotion behaviors across natural, simulated, and robotic organisms.
AI.conclusion: The approach generalizes to other physical or living systems that permit mode representation subject to geometric shape constraints, extending beyond the studied locomotion phenomena.

title: Global Existence for 3D Anisotropic MHD system with Horizontal Dissipation and Small Horizontal Variations
summary: This paper establishes the global well-posedness for the 3D anisotropic MHD system with partial dissipation: $Δ_\mathrm{h}u$ for velocity and $\partial_1^2b$ for magnetic field, near background field $(0,1,0)$. Crucially, only horizontal components $(u^\mathrm{h}_0,b^\mathrm{h}_0)$ need to be small in $H^2(\R^3)$, while $(u^3_0,b^3_0)$ can be arbitrarily large. Our analysis develops novel techniques including component-decoupled energies and iterative control of dangerous nonlinearities using the background field structure. This establishes the global result for anisotropic MHD equations allowing large vertical data, breaking the full-smallness requirement of previous works.

AI.tldr: This paper proves global well-posedness for 3D anisotropic MHD system with partial dissipation near background field (0,1,0), requiring only horizontal components to be small while vertical components can be arbitrarily large, using novel component-decoupled energies and iterative control techniques.
AI.motivation: To overcome the full-smallness requirement of previous works by allowing large vertical velocity and magnetic field components in the 3D anisotropic MHD system with partial dissipation.
AI.method: Develops component-decoupled energies and iterative control of dangerous nonlinearities using the background field structure.
AI.result: Establishes global well-posedness for 3D anisotropic MHD with partial dissipation (horizontal dissipation for velocity, vertical dissipation for magnetic field) where only horizontal components need to be small in H²(ℝ³), while vertical components can be arbitrarily large.
AI.conclusion: Successfully breaks the full-smallness constraint of prior work, establishing global results for anisotropic MHD equations allowing large vertical data.

title: Learning solution operators of PDEs with sparse approximation methods
summary: We investigate the approximation of solution operators for partial differential equations (PDEs) using sparse high-dimensional techniques. Building on a dimension-incremental framework, we combine product basis expansions with sparse recovery methods, specifically orthogonal matching pursuit (OMP), to substantially reduce the required sample size compared with a previously considered cubature-based approach. We evaluate the resulting method numerically on several examples, comparing it against both cubature-based sparse approximation and Fourier neural operators in terms of accuracy, runtime, and sample size. The experiments show that our approach considerably reduces the number of required PDE solves relative to its predecessor while maintaining competitive accuracy, particularly when the solution admits a sparse representation in the chosen basis. Furthermore, the recovered sparse index sets yield interpretable insights into the relevant variables and parameter interactions.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a sparse high-dimensional technique combining product basis expansions with orthogonal matching pursuit (OMP) to approximate PDE solution operators, reducing the required sample size compared to cubature-based methods while maintaining accuracy and providing interpretable results.
AI.motivation: To reduce the large number of PDE solves required by existing cubature-based sparse approximation methods for approximating solution operators of partial differential equations.
AI.method: Combines a dimension-incremental framework with product basis expansions and orthogonal matching pursuit (OMP) for sparse recovery, replacing a cubature-based approach.
AI.result: The proposed OMP-based approach considerably reduces the number of required PDE solves compared to cubature-based methods while maintaining competitive accuracy, especially when the solution has a sparse representation in the chosen basis.
AI.conclusion: The method achieves better sample efficiency and offers interpretable insights into relevant variables and parameter interactions, making it a promising alternative to both cubature-based sparse approximation and Fourier neural operators.

title: A Surrogate Model for Proton Spectrum Prediction to Map Transitions in Laser-Ion Acceleration
summary: We present a physics-guided, decoupled dual-branch surrogate model to predict continuous proton energy spectra from laser-driven ion acceleration. Integrating a $β$-VAE for spectral feature extraction with a parallel multi-layer perceptron for scalar boundary enforcement, the framework achieves a predictive accuracy of $R^2 = 0.94$ for the maximum cutoff energy and $R^2 = 0.94$ for the total particle flux, with a median per-sample spectral $R^2 = 0.985$ (in $\log_{10}$ space) across the full 2000-bin energy distribution. The model incorporates uncertainty quantification via deep ensembles, serving as a quantitative probabilistic diagnostic tool with calibration errors below 6.2\%. Within the 1D longitudinal framework, the surrogate reproduces spectral signatures consistent with the transition from Target Normal Sheath Acceleration (TNSA) to the volumetric heating dynamics of Relativistically Induced Transparency (RIT) and Breakout Afterburner (BOA) regimes, as validated against kinetic diagnostics from 1D particle-in-cell simulations. This approach establishes a computationally efficient baseline for future multi-fidelity optimization and provides an engine for closed-loop parameter control in high-repetition-rate laser facilities.

AI.tldr: A physics-guided dual-branch surrogate model combining β-VAE and MLP achieves high accuracy (R²=0.94-0.985) in predicting laser-driven proton energy spectra, with uncertainty quantification (calibration error <6.2%) and ability to capture TNSA-to-RIT/BOA regime transitions.
AI.motivation: To develop a computationally efficient surrogate model for predicting continuous proton energy spectra from laser-driven ion acceleration, enabling fast diagnostics and closed-loop control for high-repetition-rate laser facilities.
AI.method: A decoupled dual-branch architecture: (1) a β-VAE for spectral feature extraction, (2) a parallel MLP for scalar boundary enforcement. Uncertainty is quantified via deep ensembles. Trained and validated against 1D PIC simulation data.
AI.result: R²=0.94 for max cutoff energy and total particle flux; median per-sample spectral R²=0.985 (log₁₀ space) across 2000 bins; calibration errors below 6.2%; successfully reproduces TNSA-to-RIT/BOA transition signatures.
AI.conclusion: The model establishes a computationally efficient baseline for multi-fidelity optimization and provides an engine for closed-loop parameter control in high-repetition-rate laser facilities.

title: Predicting and controlling nonlinear neuro-mechanical locomotion dynamics
summary: Neuromechanics aims to understand the link between an animal's neural activity and its physical behaviors. Recent advances in experimental and machine learning techniques enable simultaneous recordings of neural and locomotion dynamics over long time periods and across multiple behavioral transitions in worms, flies, and other organisms. These high-dimensional datasets present the challenge of inferring interpretable low-dimensional dynamical models that quantitatively connect neural activity and behavioral dynamics. However, despite major experimental and theoretical progress, there is currently no end-to-end model for predicting locomotion and other behaviors from neural activity. Here, we present a theoretical and computational framework for inferring multiscale neuromechanical models from state-of-the-art experimental data. Our data-efficient approach combines interpretable spectral mode representations with Helmholtz-Nambu decompositions and Bayesian inference to identify a predictive stochastic model that converts neural activity time series into behavioral locomotion patterns. We first apply this framework to recently published recordings of neural activity and locomotion in the roundworm Caenorhabditis elegans, showing that it accurately describes experimentally observed dynamics. We further demonstrate how the inferred model can be used to predict neural activation patterns for controlling C. elegans locomotion in real time, providing a basis for future optogenetic experiments. Due to its generic formulation, the framework introduced here is broadly applicable to neuromechanical recordings for a wide range of animal species.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a theoretical and computational framework that combines spectral mode representations, Helmholtz-Nambu decompositions, and Bayesian inference to build predictive stochastic models converting neural activity into locomotion patterns, demonstrated on C. elegans data and enabling real-time control predictions.
AI.motivation: Despite advances in simultaneous neural and behavioral recordings, there is currently no end-to-end model for predicting locomotion and other behaviors from neural activity, motivating the need for a unified neuromechanical modeling framework.
AI.method: The framework combines interpretable spectral mode representations with Helmholtz-Nambu decompositions and Bayesian inference to infer multiscale neuromechanical models from high-dimensional experimental data.
AI.result: Applied to C. elegans neural and locomotion recordings, the model accurately describes experimentally observed dynamics and can predict neural activation patterns for real-time locomotion control.
AI.conclusion: The generic formulation makes this framework broadly applicable to neuromechanical recordings across a wide range of animal species, providing a basis for future optogenetic experiments.

title: Convergence of a discrete-in-time Approximation to a Degenerate Parabolic-Hyperbolic System
summary: In this paper we consider an implicit semi-discrete approximation of a degenerate reaction-cross-diffusion system. Due to the symmetry in the parabolic part, this system is known to preserve segregation of densities -- initially non-overlapping densities belonging to different species remain segregated for all times, which leads to internal layers between different species. We show that time-discrete approximations exist and converge to a weak solution, as the timestep goes to zero.

AI.tldr: This paper proves existence and convergence of implicit time-discrete approximations for a degenerate reaction-cross-diffusion system, showing that the segregation property (non-overlapping densities) is preserved in the limit as timestep goes to zero.
