AI.motivation: To establish rigorous mathematical foundations for numerical approximation of degenerate reaction-cross-diffusion systems that exhibit density segregation and internal layer formation between different species.
AI.method: An implicit semi-discrete (time-discrete) approximation scheme is constructed for the degenerate reaction-cross-diffusion system. The analysis leverages the symmetry structure in the parabolic part to preserve the segregation property at each time step, then proves convergence as the timestep approaches zero.
AI.result: The time-discrete approximations exist and converge to a weak solution in the vanishing timestep limit, while maintaining the segregation property throughout the approximation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed implicit scheme successfully approximates weak solutions of the degenerate reaction-cross-diffusion system while preserving the physically/biologically relevant segregation property, providing a theoretically sound numerical framework.

title: Residual-based Kaczmarz methods for tensor linear equations with t-product
summary: Tensor linear systems widely arise from high-dimensional data mining and computing, for instance, natural language processing and machine learning. A class of residual-based tensor Kaczmarz method is proposed for tensor linear equations with t-product. Theoretical analyses prove the convergence and give an upper bound of the convergence rate of the proposed method. Furthermore, an accelerated residual-based Kaczmarz method with heavy ball momentum is developed. Numerical experiments verify the efficiency of the proposed methods and demonstrate that they are faster than the existing tensor Kaczmarz methods.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes residual-based tensor Kaczmarz methods for solving tensor linear systems, including an accelerated version with heavy ball momentum, proving convergence and showing improved efficiency over existing methods.
AI.motivation: Tensor linear systems are widely used in high-dimensional data mining, natural language processing, and machine learning, motivating the need for efficient solution methods.
AI.method: 1) Proposes a class of residual-based tensor Kaczmarz methods for tensor linear equations with t-product. 2) Develops an accelerated version with heavy ball momentum.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis proves convergence and provides an upper bound on the convergence rate. Numerical experiments show the proposed methods are faster than existing tensor Kaczmarz methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed residual-based tensor Kaczmarz methods, especially with heavy ball momentum, are efficient and converge faster than existing approaches for solving tensor linear systems.

title: Plasma wakefield dynamics of self-generated electron bunch trains
summary: Laser plasma accelerators can deliver high-energy, quasi-monoenergetic electron beams over centimeter-scale distances. In this work, we report on the generation of narrow, quasi-monoenergetic electron bunch trains with periodic energy spacing issued from downramp injection in a laser driven wakefield accelerator. The periodicity in energy is shaped via relativistic lengthening of the wakefield during the acceleration phase, while the spatial periodicity is obtained via injection into multiple plasma periods. At the end of the accelerator, a rotation in phase-space is performed to compress each bunch in energy, producing narrow periodic spikes in the spectrum. The experimental observations are supported by particle-in-cell simulations, which reproduce the formation and evolution of the periodic bunch trains, providing an insight into the underlying plasma dynamics.

AI.tldr: This paper reports the generation of narrow, quasi-monoenergetic electron bunch trains with periodic energy spacing using downramp injection in a laser-driven wakefield accelerator, supported by simulations.
AI.motivation: To generate electron bunch trains with periodic energy spacing from laser plasma accelerators, which could benefit applications requiring structured electron beams.
AI.method: Downramp injection in a laser-driven wakefield accelerator with relativistic wakefield lengthening, multi-period injection, and phase-space rotation for energy compression.
AI.result: Successful generation of narrow, quasi-monoenergetic electron bunch trains with periodic energy spacing, reproduced in PIC simulations.
AI.conclusion: The periodic bunch train formation is achievable via the proposed mechanism, and simulations provide insight into the underlying plasma dynamics.

title: xCPS: an xAct package for covariant phase space, Noether charges, and entropy
summary: xCPS is an xAct tensor algebra package for symbolic computations within the covariant phase space formalism of field theories. From a generic Lagrangian, xCPS automates the derivation of equations of motion and symplectic currents. It systematically determines whether an infinitesimal transformation in the space of fields is a Noether symmetry and computes the associated Noether charge. Additionally, xCPS can in many cases determine whether a tensorial expression is a divergence and, if so, find its divergence potential. By implementing vertical exterior calculus through a graded, supercommutative wedge product and vertical operators, the package enables efficient computations in gauge theories and higher-derivative models of gravity, including the derivation of thermodynamic quantities like Wald's entropy. xCPS is open-source under the GPL license and available at https://github.com/juanmargalef.

AI.tldr: xCPS is an xAct tensor algebra package for automating covariant phase space computations, including equations of motion, Noether symmetries/charges, divergence potentials, and Wald entropy from any Lagrangian.
AI.motivation: To automate symbolic computations in the covariant phase space formalism, reducing manual effort for complex gauge theories and higher-derivative gravity models.
AI.method: Implements vertical exterior calculus with graded supercommutative wedge products and vertical operators within the xAct tensor algebra framework.
AI.result: Automates derivation of equations of motion, symplectic currents, Noether symmetries/charges, divergence potentials, and thermodynamic quantities like Wald entropy.
AI.conclusion: xCPS provides an efficient, open-source tool for covariant phase space computations, particularly useful for gauge theories and higher-derivative gravity.

title: A Pohozaev-type neck proof of a conditional Harnack inequality in the critical $p$-Laplacian setting
summary:
We prove a conditional Schoen-type Harnack inequality for positive weak solutions of the critical $p$-Laplace equation $$
  -Δ_p u=g(u),\qquad 1<p<n, $$ under a global critical Sobolev growth assumption and the monotonicity condition that $s^{-(p^*-1)}g(s)$ is nonincreasing. The result is conditional on two inputs, the classification of bounded positive entire blow-up limits as Aubin--Talenti $p$-bubbles and a preliminary singular-rate upper control on the normalized necks. Under these two hypotheses, solutions in $B_{3R}$ satisfy $$
  \Big(\sup_{B_R}u\Big)\Big(\inf_{B_{2R}}u\Big)^{p-1}
  \le C R^{p-n}. $$ The main point is a Pohozaev-neck argument which upgrades the preliminary singular decay rate $|x|^{-(n-p)/p}$ to the sharp $p$-harmonic fundamental rate $|x|^{-(n-p)/(p-1)}$. The argument replaces the Kelvin-transform and moving-sphere methods available in the conformally invariant semilinear case $p=2$, but unavailable for the general $p$-Laplacian.

AI.tldr: The paper proves a conditional Harnack inequality for positive weak solutions of the critical p-Laplace equation, upgrading a singular decay rate to the sharp p-harmonic fundamental rate via a Pohozaev-neck argument that circumvents the unavailable Kelvin-transform and moving-sphere methods for general p.
AI.motivation: To establish a Harnack-type inequality for the critical p-Laplace equation when p≠2, where classical methods (Kelvin transform, moving spheres) that work for the semilinear case p=2 are unavailable.
AI.method: The authors develop a Pohozaev-neck argument that upgrades a preliminary singular decay rate |x|^{-(n-p)/p} to the sharp p-harmonic fundamental rate |x|^{-(n-p)/(p-1)}, conditional on: (1) classification of bounded positive entire blow-up limits as Aubin-Talenti p-bubbles, and (2) a preliminary singular-rate upper control on normalized necks.
AI.result: Under the two hypotheses, solutions in B_{3R} satisfy sup_{B_R} u * (inf_{B_{2R}} u)^{p-1} ≤ C R^{p-n}.
AI.conclusion: The Pohozaev-neck argument successfully replaces unavailable conformal methods for general p, achieving the sharp decay rate for the p-Laplacian case.

title: Weak order one convergence of structure-preserving stochastic theta methods for stochastic differential algebraic equations with time-dependent singular matrices
summary: This paper studies the weak convergence order of structure-preserving stochastic theta methods for a class of index-$1$ stochastic differential algebraic equations with time-dependent singular matrices. The singular matrix is allowed to vary in time but preserves a fixed differential-algebraic splitting, thereby extending the constant singular-matrix setting while retaining the projector structure required for constraint preservation. By exploiting the index-$1$ algebraic-differential decomposition of the exact solution, we establish an abstract weak convergence theorem for constraint-preserving one-step approximations and apply it to the stochastic theta method with $θ\in (0,1]$. Under global Lipschitz, linear growth, and suitable smoothness assumptions, the considered method is proved to be well posed, to preserve the algebraic constraints at all time levels, and to converge with weak order one. Numerical experiments are finally presented to confirm the structure-preserving property and the theoretical convergence order.

AI.tldr: This paper proves weak order one convergence for structure-preserving stochastic theta methods applied to index-1 stochastic differential algebraic equations with time-dependent singular matrices, under Lipschitz and smoothness conditions.
AI.motivation: To extend weak convergence analysis from constant singular-matrix settings to time-dependent singular matrices for SDAEs, while preserving algebraic constraints and projector structures.
AI.method: The authors exploit the index-1 algebraic-differential decomposition of the exact solution, establish an abstract weak convergence theorem for constraint-preserving one-step approximations, and apply it to the stochastic theta method with θ ∈ (0,1].
AI.result: The stochastic theta method is well-posed, preserves algebraic constraints at all time levels, and achieves weak order one convergence under global Lipschitz, linear growth, and smoothness assumptions.
AI.conclusion: The theoretical weak order one is confirmed by numerical experiments, validating the structure-preserving property.

title: Nonthermal Particle Acceleration by Magnetic Pumping in Pulsating Plasmas
summary: We present a new "pulsating box" setup to investigate particle acceleration in high-beta plasmas undergoing compression-expansion cycles. Our fully kinetic simulations show that particles are efficiently accelerated by magnetic pumping, producing nonthermal energy distributions with power-law tails. Numerical results are in excellent agreement with a generalized maximum entropy model that we derive, linking the power-law index of distributions to the injected energy. Our results are relevant for understanding the origin of high-energy particles in space and astrophysical plasmas.

AI.tldr: A pulsating box simulation setup shows particles are efficiently accelerated via magnetic pumping, producing power-law nonthermal energy distributions that match a generalized maximum entropy model linking the power-law index to injected energy.
AI.motivation: To understand the origin of high-energy particles in space and astrophysical plasmas by investigating particle acceleration in high-beta plasmas undergoing compression-expansion cycles.
AI.method: A "pulsating box" setup with fully kinetic simulations to study particle acceleration in high-beta plasmas under compression-expansion cycles.
AI.result: Particles are efficiently accelerated by magnetic pumping, producing nonthermal energy distributions with power-law tails. Results match a generalized maximum entropy model.
AI.conclusion: The findings are relevant for understanding high-energy particle origins in space and astrophysical plasmas.

title: Tricriticality and chaos in a generalized Allee-logistic map
summary: We present a novel nonlinear dynamical model, the generalized Allee-logistic (GAL) map given by $x_{t+1} = r x_t (1 - x_t) G(x_t)$ where $G(x_t) = m (x_t - h) + 1 - m$ incorporates the Allee effect with magnitude $m$ and threshold $h$. The case $m = 0$ yields the logistic map with a continuous transition to extinction. Conversely, $m = 1$ recovers a previously studied model that undergoes only a discontinuous extinction-to-active transition. Between these extremes, the GAL map exhibits nontrivial phenomena, including tricriticality with a closed-form expression for the tricritical point and a universal crossover function. Under a small external input, we verify Widom-like relations. We also note that the Allee effect disfavors the onset of chaos. Our work establishes additional bridges between analytically tractable chaotic maps, nonequilibrium tricriticality, and Allee effects.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the generalized Allee-logistic (GAL) map, a nonlinear dynamical model that interpolates between the standard logistic map and an Allee-effect model, revealing tricriticality, universal crossover behavior, and Widom-like relations, while showing that the Allee effect suppresses chaos.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between analytically tractable chaotic maps, nonequilibrium tricriticality, and Allee effects by introducing a tunable model that captures both continuous and discontinuous extinction-to-active transitions.
AI.method: The authors propose the GAL map $x_{t+1} = r x_t (1 - x_t) G(x_t)$ with $G(x_t) = m (x_t - h) + 1 - m$, where $m$ controls the Allee effect strength and $h$ is the threshold. They analyze its bifurcation structure analytically and numerically across the parameter space.
AI.result: The model exhibits tricriticality with a closed-form tricritical point and a universal crossover function. Widom-like relations are verified under small external input. The Allee effect is shown to disfavor the onset of chaos.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes new analytical connections between chaotic maps, nonequilibrium tricritical phenomena, and ecological Allee effects, offering a tractable framework for studying complex transitions.

title: Preventing $L^p$ blow-up by local anisotropy of signal production in the Keller-Segel system with strongly differing diffusion rates
summary:
In a smoothly bounded domain $Ω\subset R^n$, $n\le 5$, the manuscript considers the variant of the Keller-Segel system given by \[
  \left\{ \begin{array}{l} 
  u_t = D Δu - \nabla \cdot (u\nabla v), \\[1mm]
  v_t = d Δv + \nabla \cdot (u\nabla v) - v + u,
  \end{array} \right. \] which involves an additional contribution $\nabla \cdot (u\nabla v)$ to the chemoattractant evolution, in line with refined modeling literature reflecting an anisotropic correction to the isotropic signal production term $+u$ in the classical Keller-Segel model.
  It is shown that for arbitrary $D>0$ and $d>0$ and any nonnegative intial data from $W^{1,\infty}(Ω)\times W^{1, \infty}(Ω)$, an associated Neumann problem admits a global weak solution $(u,v)$ which, inter alia, satisfies \[
  \sup_{t \in (0,\infty)\setminus N} \int_Ωe^{u^α(\cdot,t)} < \infty \] with some $α>0$ and some null set $N\subset (0,\infty)$.

AI.tldr: This paper proves global weak solution existence for a modified Keller-Segel system with an additional chemotactic cross-diffusion term in the signal equation, showing exponential integrability of cell density in domains up to dimension 5.
AI.motivation: To study a refined Keller-Segel model incorporating an anisotropic correction term ∇·(u∇v) in the chemoattractant equation, reflecting more accurate biological modeling of signal production and transport.
AI.method: Mathematical analysis using PDE theory, establishing global weak solution existence for the Neumann problem with initial data in W^{1,∞}, and proving exponential integrability bounds for the cell density u.
AI.result: For any D>0, d>0, and n≤5, global weak solutions exist with sup_t ∫_Ω e^{u^α} < ∞ for some α>0, showing strong regularity properties.
AI.conclusion: The modified Keller-Segel system with the anisotropic correction term admits global weak solutions with exponential integrability, extending existing theory to this refined model.

title: A tensor-train multidimensional inverse Laplace transform
summary: Laplace transforms and their numerical inverses arise throughout applied mathematics, physics, finance, and probability theory. Numerical inversion, however, quickly becomes intractable in high dimensions because the number of quadrature evaluations grows exponentially with dimension. We develop a tensor train (TT) formulation of the multidimensional inverse Laplace transform. The method constructs a TT approximation of the transformed function on the complex quadrature grid and then performs the inversion through a sequence of tensor contractions. Under suitable low-rank assumptions, this reduces the computational cost from exponential to polynomial in the dimension, provided that the relevant bond dimensions remain bounded. The method has only a small number of tunable parameters and admits error estimations. We demonstrate its performance in numerical experiments, benchmarked against Monte Carlo estimates and exact references, for multivariate normal-inverse Gaussian, Wishart, and correlated Gamma-type distributions.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a tensor train (TT) formulation for the multidimensional inverse Laplace transform, reducing computational cost from exponential to polynomial in dimension under low-rank assumptions.
AI.motivation: Numerical inversion of Laplace transforms becomes intractable in high dimensions because quadrature evaluations grow exponentially with dimension.
AI.method: The method constructs a TT approximation of the transformed function on the complex quadrature grid and performs inversion through tensor contractions, exploiting low-rank structure.
AI.result: Benchmarked against Monte Carlo and exact references for multivariate normal-inverse Gaussian, Wishart, and correlated Gamma-type distributions, demonstrating performance improvements.
AI.conclusion: The TT approach reduces cost from exponential to polynomial in dimension, has few tunable parameters, and admits error estimation.

title: Minor Ions as a Diagnostic of Solar Wind Heating: Inverted Mass-to-Charge Scaling in Imbalanced Turbulence
summary: Alfvénic turbulence is vital to powering the solar wind and corona, yet eludes a comprehensive understanding of the kinetic processes by which it dissipates. Minor ions are sensitive tracers of these processes, showing extreme perpendicular temperatures and mass-weighted temperature trends that can either correlate or anticorrelate with mass-to-charge ratio, $A_i/Z_i$. We use a combination of quasilinear theory and 3D hybrid-kinetic simulations to explain these features and their correlations with properties of turbulence in the fast solar wind. When Alfvénic turbulence is imbalanced, its cascade to ion-Larmor scales is throttled by the helicity barrier. This barrier ultimately leads to high-frequency proton-cyclotron waves (PCWs), both oblique and parallel, the latter of which produce very flat electric-energy spectra ($\mathcal{E}_{E_{\perp}}\sim k_\parallel^{-η}$ with $η<2$) over the range of scales that are cyclotron resonant with minor ions. While steeper spectra lead to a positive correlation of heating with $A_i/Z_i$, the shallower spectra cause the dependence to invert, with $Q_i\propto Q_{\mathrm{p}}A_i(A_i/Z_i)^{η-2}$. Six simulations of balanced and imbalanced turbulence spanning $β_{\rm p0}=\{1,0.3,1/16\}$ corroborate this prediction, showing minor-ion heating rates that follow $(A_i/Z_i)^a$. Minor-ion heating is strongest and most perpendicular in our lowest $β_{\rm p0}=1/16$ simulation of imbalanced turbulence, reaching $T_{\perp{\rm O}^{5+}}/T_{\perp{\rm p}}\approx40$ and $T_{\perp{\rm O}^{5+}}/T_{\parallel{\rm O}^{5+}}\approx10$, consistent with low-coronal observations. Future minor-ion measurements should test whether intervals in which minor-ion thermal speeds decrease with increasing mass-to-charge ratio are associated with a history of large cross helicity, enhanced power in parallel PCWs, and a steep transition-range spectrum.

AI.tldr: This paper explains how Alfvénic turbulence dissipates in the solar wind, focusing on the helicity barrier that produces proton-cyclotron waves causing minor ions to heat with temperature trends that can either correlate or anticorrelate with mass-to-charge ratio, depending on wave spectrum steepness.
AI.motivation: To understand the kinetic processes by which Alfvénic turbulence dissipates and powers the solar wind/corona, using minor ions as sensitive tracers.
AI.method: Combination of quasilinear theory and 3D hybrid-kinetic simulations of balanced and imbalanced turbulence across different plasma beta values.
AI.result: When turbulence is imbalanced, the helicity barrier throttles the cascade, producing PCWs with flat spectra (η<2) that invert the heating-mass/charge correlation; simulations show minor-ion heating follows (A_i/Z_i)^a, with strongest heating at low β_p0.
AI.conclusion: Future minor-ion measurements should test whether intervals of decreasing thermal speed with mass-to-charge ratio associate with large cross helicity and parallel PCW power.

title: Learning and Inferring Multiphase Flow Dynamics in Porous Media using Scientific Machine Learning: Application to the "FluidFlower" CO2 Injection Experiment
summary: Accurate prediction and parameter identification of multiphase flow in porous media remain central challenges in geological carbon dioxide storage due to strong nonlinearities, high-dimensional parameter spaces, and limited observational data. We present a machine learning framework that integrates surrogate modeling and Bayesian inference to enable efficient forward prediction and inverse parameter estimation for CO2-brine flows in geological media. The approach is demonstrated using the "FluidFlower" experimental rig, a controlled laboratory system that provides high-resolution, time-resolved observations of CO2 migration in heterogeneous porous media. A convolutional neural network surrogate is trained on high-fidelity numerical simulations to learn the evolution of CO2 saturation and dissolved CO2 concentration fields over a wide range of multiphase flow properties. The trained surrogate is embedded within a Markov chain Monte Carlo framework for parameter inference conditioned on experimental observations. Results show that the surrogate accurately captures large-scale CO2 plume migration, dissolution dynamics, and multiphase flow behavior while providing orders-of-magnitude acceleration compared to traditional simulations. Embedding the surrogate within a Bayesian framework enables computationally tractable exploration of the parameter space and reveals both identifiable and non-identifiable parameter combinations that produce similar plume behavior. By leveraging spatially and temporally resolved full-field observations, the framework substantially improves agreement between simulations and experiments compared to previous manual calibrations based on limited plume-scale metrics. Analysis using progressively increasing observation horizons further shows that observations become more informative once the plume interacts with geological features such as faults and sealing layers.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a machine learning framework combining surrogate modeling (CNN) with Bayesian inference (MCMC) for efficient forward prediction and inverse parameter estimation of CO2-brine flows in porous media, validated using the "FluidFlower" experimental rig. The surrogate achieves orders-of-magnitude speedup while accurately capturing plume migration and dissolution dynamics, and enables identification of both identifiable and non-identifiable parameter combinations using full-field observations.
AI.motivation: Accurate prediction and parameter identification of multiphase flow in porous media is challenging due to strong nonlinearities, high-dimensional parameter spaces, and limited observational data, which are critical for geological CO2 storage.
AI.method: A convolutional neural network (CNN) surrogate is trained on high-fidelity numerical simulations to learn CO2 saturation and dissolved concentration evolution. The surrogate is embedded in a Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) framework for Bayesian inference, conditioned on experimental observations from the "FluidFlower" rig.
AI.result: The surrogate provides orders-of-magnitude acceleration while accurately capturing large-scale CO2 plume migration and dissolution dynamics. The Bayesian framework reveals identifiable and non-identifiable parameter combinations. Full-field observations substantially improve simulation-experiment agreement over manual calibrations. Observations become more informative when plumes interact with faults and sealing layers.
AI.conclusion: Integrating surrogate modeling with Bayesian inference enables computationally tractable and accurate parameter estimation for multiphase flow, and leveraging spatiotemporally resolved observations significantly enhances predictive fidelity for geological CO2 storage applications.

title: Optimal decay for waves damped by superellipses
summary: Energy decay rates for solutions of the damped wave equation on the torus are known to be influenced by the geometry of the damped set and the growth properties of the damping. In this paper we produce lower bounds on energy decay rates for a class of damping which are positive on a superellipse and grow polynomially like the distance to the boundary of the superellipse. The energy decay rates we obtain depend explicitly on the exponent used to define the superellipse and the polynomial power. We show these rates are sometimes optimal. The proof adapts quasimodes from $y$-invariant damping using a simplification of the usual normal form argument.

AI.tldr: This paper studies energy decay rates for the damped wave equation on a torus, focusing on damping that is positive on a superellipse and grows polynomially near its boundary. The authors derive explicit lower bounds depending on the superellipse exponent and polynomial power, prove optimality in some cases, and adapt quasimodes using a simplified normal form argument.
AI.motivation: To understand how the geometry of the damped set and the growth properties of the damping influence the energy decay rates for solutions of damped wave equations on the torus.
AI.method: The proof adapts quasimodes from y-invariant damping using a simplification of the usual normal form argument.
AI.result: Lower bounds on energy decay rates are derived that depend explicitly on the superellipse exponent and the polynomial power. These rates are shown to be optimal in some cases.
AI.conclusion: The energy decay rates depend explicitly on the superellipse exponent and polynomial power, and are sometimes optimal; the method uses a simplified normal form argument.

title: A Microlocal Open-Boundary Method for Residual-Based Wave Solvers on Unbounded Domains
summary:
We introduce a microlocal phase-space-filtered physics-informed neural network (PINN--TDPSF or Microlocal PINNFilter) framework for wave propagation on unbounded domains. The method combines a slabwise neural residual approximation of the interior evolution with a time-dependent phase-space filter applied in a buffer surrounding the physical computational domain. The central idea is to replace local artificial-boundary penalties by a phase-space radiation mechanism: a component is removed only when it is localized near the artificial boundary and its group velocity points outward.
  The proposed method is not intended to replace FFT, spectral, or split-step solvers for known-coefficient forward problems where such methods are available and highly accurate. Instead, it embeds the time-dependent phase-space filter into a residual-based neural framework. This coupling is useful when open-domain wave propagation must be combined with nonlinear residuals, sparse or off-grid observations, unknown coefficients, variable interior media, or other non-FFT-diagonalizable physics. Numerical experiments for linear Schrödinger propagation, potential scattering, anisotropic Schrödinger dynamics, nonlinear Schrödinger wave packets, soliton stress tests, linearized Euler waves, and sparse-data recovery of a localized acoustic defect show that the method reduces artificial reflection and wraparound, uses group velocity correctly in anisotropic media, preserves physically incoming branch components, and provides diagnostics when the assumptions behind outgoing-packet filtering are violated.

AI.tldr: Microlocal PINNFilter combines physics-informed neural networks with time-dependent phase-space filtering to reduce artificial reflections at boundaries for wave propagation on unbounded domains, demonstrating effectiveness across linear/nonlinear Schrödinger, Euler, and acoustic defect recovery problems.
AI.motivation: Standard artificial boundary conditions cause reflections and wraparound errors when simulating wave propagation on unbounded domains. Existing methods like FFT or spectral solvers are highly accurate for known-coefficient forward problems but fail when nonlinear residuals, unknown coefficients, sparse/off-grid observations, or anisotropic media are involved.
AI.method: Combines a slabwise neural residual approximation of interior wave evolution with a time-dependent phase-space filter in a buffer zone. The filter removes wave components only when they are localized near the artificial boundary and their group velocity points outward, effectively replacing local artificial-boundary penalties with a radiation mechanism.
AI.result: Validated on linear Schrödinger propagation, potential scattering, anisotropic Schrödinger dynamics, nonlinear Schrödinger wave packets, soliton stress tests, linearized Euler waves, and sparse-data recovery of localized acoustic defects. The method reduces artificial reflection and wraparound, correctly handles group velocity in anisotropic media, preserves physically incoming branches, and provides diagnostic signals when outgoing-packet filtering assumptions break down.
AI.conclusion: The Microlocal PINNFilter is a useful coupling framework for open-domain wave propagation problems that are not easily diagonalizable by FFT or spectral methods, particularly when nonlinearity, unknown coefficients, anisotropic media, or sparse data are involved.

title: Depolarization and Polarization-Transfer Rates for Solar He I Lines due to Collisions with Neutral Hydrogen
comment: Accepted for publication in Astronomy \& Astrophysics
summary:
Context. Neutral helium (He I) produces several spectral lines that are widely used for solar diagnostics. The role of collisions between He I atoms and neutral hydrogen (H I) in the modeling of solar He I lines remains insufficiently quantified. Accurate determination of collisional rates affecting atomic polarization is needed for solar spectropolarimetry.
  Aims. Our aim is to provide a set of multi-level and multi-term collisional depolarization, polarization-transfer, and population-transfer rates, due to isotropic collisions with neutral hydrogen, for He I levels and terms involved in the main solar He I diagnostic lines.
  Methods. The calculations are performed within the frozen-core approximation, in which the inner 1s electron is treated as a core with Lc = 0, Sc = 1/2, and Jc = 1/2, while the outer electron is treated as the active valence electron.
  Results. We compute both multi-level rates, describing depolarization and polarization transfer between fine-structure J-levels, and multi-term rates, which additionally account for coherences between different J-levels belonging to the same term.
  Conclusions. Our results provide the collisional input needed for the statistical equilibrium equations (SEE) of the polarization of the main He I solar lines, including the 10830 Å, D3 5876 Å, and related triplet transitions, and allow a quantitative reassessment of the role of neutral-hydrogen collisions in He I spectropolarimetry.

AI.tldr: This paper computes collisional rates between He I and neutral hydrogen for modeling solar He I spectral lines, providing multi-level and multi-term rates for depolarization, polarization-transfer, and population-transfer needed for solar spectropolarimetry.
AI.motivation: The role of He I-H I collisions in modeling solar He I lines is insufficiently quantified, and accurate collisional rates affecting atomic polarization are needed for solar spectropolarimetry.
AI.method: Calculations use the frozen-core approximation, treating the inner 1s electron as a core (Lc=0, Sc=1/2, Jc=1/2) and the outer electron as the active valence electron, computing both multi-level and multi-term rates.
AI.result: Multi-level rates (depolarization and polarization transfer between fine-structure J-levels) and multi-term rates (accounting for coherences between J-levels within the same term) are computed for He I levels involved in solar diagnostic lines.
AI.conclusion: The rates provide the collisional input for statistical equilibrium equations of polarization in main He I solar lines (10830 Å, D3 5876 Å, and related triplet transitions), enabling quantitative reassessment of neutral-hydrogen collisions in He I spectropolarimetry.

title: Small deformations of a near cylindrical tube for the Canham-Helfrich Energy with applications to biological membranes
summary: In this article we develop a quadratic energy which approximates the Canham-Helfrich energy for a tube-like surface with clamped boundary and area constraint. The energy is suited to the study of small deformations of biological membranes where the deformations are induced by point forces or point constraints due to the cytoskeleton or a phase dependent spontaneous curvature. Since the deformations we consider are small, we may assume that the surface of interest is a graph over a fixed, undeformed surface. A Lagrangian and the associated Euler-Lagrange equations for the graph are derived. Well-posedness of the Euler-Lagrange equations in suitable spaces is shown. Finally, we provide some illustrative numerical examples.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a quadratic energy approximating the Canham-Helfrich model for tube-like biological membranes under small deformations, deriving Euler-Lagrange equations and showing well-posedness with numerical examples.
AI.motivation: To study small deformations of biological membranes induced by point forces or constraints from the cytoskeleton or phase-dependent spontaneous curvature.
AI.method: Develops a quadratic energy approximating the Canham-Helfrich energy for tube-like surfaces with clamped boundary and area constraint, treats the surface as a graph over a fixed undeformed surface, derives Lagrangian and Euler-Lagrange equations.
AI.result: Shows well-posedness of the Euler-Lagrange equations in suitable function spaces and provides illustrative numerical examples.
AI.conclusion: The proposed quadratic energy model is suitable for analyzing small deformations of biological membranes under localized mechanical influences.

title: Ensemble Kalman Inversion as an Inertial Interacting Particle System
summary:
Ensemble Kalman Inversion (EKI) is a derivative-free, ensemble-based method for inverse and optimization problems. Its continuous-time formulation can be interpreted as an interacting particle system driven by a Kalman-type preconditioned descent direction. A well-known limitation of this dynamics is the possible premature collapse of the covariance of the ensemble, which makes the method sensitive to the initial ensemble.
  We introduce a second-order particle system in which the particles evolve according to an inertial dynamics. The model combines a Kalman-type relaxation force with damping, attraction towards the ensemble mean, and a short-range repulsive interaction designed to counteract ensemble collapse. The resulting dynamics can be interpreted as a heavy-ball reformulation of continuous-time EKI enriched by competing attractive and repulsive mechanisms.
  For linear inverse problems, we analyze the induced mean and fluctuation dynamics and identify a parameter regime in which fully collapsed configurations are linearly unstable. We further characterize asymptotic equilibria through a constrained optimality condition on the subspace retained by the limiting ensemble covariance and derive an exponential decay estimate. Numerical experiments illustrate the effect of inertia and repulsion on the ensemble dynamics and compare the proposed second-order method with first-order EKI-type

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a second-order (inertial) particle system for Ensemble Kalman Inversion that adds damping, mean-attraction, and repulsive forces to prevent the premature covariance collapse that limits standard EKI methods.
AI.motivation: Standard continuous-time EKI suffers from premature collapse of the ensemble covariance, making the method highly sensitive to the initial ensemble choice.
AI.method: A second-order particle system with inertial dynamics is proposed, combining Kalman-type relaxation, damping, attraction to the ensemble mean, and short-range repulsive interactions to prevent collapse. This is framed as a heavy-ball reformulation of continuous-time EKI.
AI.result: For linear inverse problems, the paper identifies a parameter regime where fully collapsed configurations are linearly unstable, characterizes asymptotic equilibria via constrained optimality, and derives exponential decay estimates.
AI.conclusion: The proposed second-order method with inertia and repulsion effectively counteracts ensemble collapse, as demonstrated through numerical experiments comparing it to first-order EKI-type methods.

title: On the training of physics-informed neural operators for solving parametric partial differential equations
summary: Physics-informed neural operators (PINOs) aim to learn solution operators for partial differential equations by using the governing physics as supervision, rather than relying solely on paired input-output simulation data. By incorporating physical constraints into the training objective, PINOs combine the cross-instance generalization of neural operators with the data efficiency of physics-informed learning. Despite this promise, how to train PINOs efficiently and robustly remains less well-understood than the training of either data-driven neural operators or physics-informed neural networks (PINNs). To bridge this gap, we examine key components of the PINO training pipeline, including architecture design, optimizer choice, loss balancing, and collocation-point sampling strategy. We study three representative operator backbones, Deep Operator Network (DeepONet), Fourier Neural Operator (FNO), and Continuous Vision Transformer (CViT), across five diverse parametric PDE systems. Our results show that CViT provides consistently strong and stable performance across the considered benchmarks. Beyond architecture, we find that several optimization pathologies previously identified in PINN training naturally arise in PINOs, including gradient conflicts and causal violation. We also find that mitigation algorithms developed for PINNs remain effective in the PINO setting. We further compare physics-informed and data-driven training under different data regimes, revealing that a carefully designed physics-informed training pipeline can match, and in some cases, outperform purely data-driven neural operators. Taken together, these findings provide a systematic empirical understanding of the optimization challenges in PINO training and inform a practical pipeline for efficient and robust physics-informed operator learning. Code and data are available at https://github.com/NanxiiChen/PI-CViT.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically studies the training of Physics-Informed Neural Operators (PINOs), examining architecture (DeepONet, FNO, CViT), optimizer choice, loss balancing, and sampling strategies across five PDE systems. Key findings: CViT consistently outperforms other backbones; PINOs suffer from similar optimization pathologies as PINNs (gradient conflicts, causal violation); physics-informed training can match or outperform data-driven approaches with proper pipeline design.
AI.motivation: Training PINOs efficiently and robustly is not well-understood compared to data-driven neural operators or PINNs. The authors aim to bridge this gap by systematically examining key components of the PINO training pipeline.
AI.method: The authors examine architecture design (DeepONet, FNO, CViT), optimizer choice, loss balancing, and collocation-point sampling strategies across five diverse parametric PDE systems. They compare physics-informed vs data-driven training under different data regimes.
AI.result: CViT provides consistently strong and stable performance. PINOs exhibit optimization pathologies (gradient conflicts, causal violation) similar to PINNs. Mitigation algorithms from PINNs remain effective for PINOs. A well-designed physics-informed pipeline can match or outperform purely data-driven neural operators.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide systematic empirical understanding of optimization challenges in PINO training and inform a practical pipeline for efficient and robust physics-informed operator learning.

title: Local and global properties of solutions of an elliptic equation involving exponential and gradient reaction
summary: We study some local and global properties of solutions of $-Δu- m\abs{\nabla u}^q-e^{u}=0$ in a punctured domain $Ω\setminus\{0\}$, or in an exterior domain of $R^N$, $N\geq 2$, where $m$ is a positive parameter and $q>1$. We study particularly the local behaviour of solutions with an isolated singularity or the asymptotic behaviour for solutions defined in an exterior domain, and also the existence of solutions with the behaviours previously described. These behaviours change drastically according $q$ is smaller or larger than $2$. Many results are obtained by introducing various dynamical systems associated to the equation.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes local/global properties of solutions to a semilinear elliptic equation with gradient term and exponential nonlinearity in punctured/exterior domains, focusing on how behavior changes when q<2 vs q>2, using dynamical systems approaches.
AI.motivation: To understand how the parameter q (relative to 2) drastically changes solution behaviors near isolated singularities and at infinity for this equation with a gradient-dependent nonlinearity.
AI.method: The authors introduce dynamical systems associated to the semilinear elliptic equation to study isolated singularities and asymptotic behavior in exterior domains.
AI.result: Solution behaviors (local near singularity, asymptotic at infinity) change drastically depending on whether q<2 or q>2, with existence results for each regime.
AI.conclusion: The threshold q=2 is critical; below and above it produce qualitatively different solution profiles, effectively characterized via dynamical systems.

title: Post-processed frozen-flow methods for the long time sampling of ergodic dynamics on Riemannian manifolds
summary: In this work, we propose a novel intrinsic approach to the approximation of ergodic SDEs on Riemannian manifolds, which include Riemannian Langevin dynamics. In opposition to the standard extrinsic approaches such as penalization methods and projection methods, our methodology does not use embeddings or coordinates and only relies on natural geometric operations: geodesics, parallel transport,... We give a criterion for high order of accuracy for the invariant measure, develop new intrinsic numerical methods designed solely for sampling the invariant measure, and derive high order conditions using a new algebraic operation on exotic Lie-Butcher series. In the spirit of the Leimkuhler-Matthews method, our approach prioritizes long time sampling efficiency over finite time accuracy, and outperforms the previous extrinsic and intrinsic approaches in terms of cost for a given accuracy, which we illustrate with several numerical experiments.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a new intrinsic numerical method for sampling ergodic SDEs on Riemannian manifolds (e.g., Riemannian Langevin dynamics) using only geometric operations like geodesics and parallel transport, achieving higher order accuracy for invariant measures and outperforming existing extrinsic and intrinsic approaches in sampling efficiency.
AI.motivation: Standard extrinsic methods (penalization, projection) rely on embeddings or coordinates, which can be inefficient or geometrically unnatural. The authors aim to develop a purely intrinsic approach that respects the manifold's geometry while achieving high-order accuracy for invariant measure sampling.
AI.method: The authors develop intrinsic numerical schemes using geodesics and parallel transport, derive high-order conditions via exotic Lie-Butcher series algebra, and design methods prioritizing long-time sampling efficiency (inspired by Leimkuhler-Matthews) over finite-time accuracy.
AI.result: The intrinsic methods outperform both previous extrinsic and intrinsic approaches in cost-for-accuracy trade-offs, as demonstrated by numerical experiments on Riemannian manifolds.
AI.conclusion: A purely geometric, coordinate-free intrinsic approach can achieve superior sampling efficiency for ergodic SDEs on manifolds, opening new directions for manifold sampling methods.

title: Effective Dimensionality as an Operator Invariant for Physics-Preserving Constraint Adaptation in Physics-Informed Neural Networks
summary: Physics-Informed Neural Networks inherently suffer from task interference because they rely on a shared parameter space to satisfy both governing differential equations and boundary conditions. We analyze this structural conflict using the Fisher Information Matrix to quantify the effective degrees of freedom ($d_{eff}$) in a physics-constrained model. Unlike the classical $d_{eff}$ which measures how many parameter directions are informed by data against a statistical prior, our $d_{eff}$ measures the dimension of the parameter directions unconstrained by the differential operator. For operators with finite-dimensional kernel, we show that $d_{eff}$ converges to the kernel dimension exactly, independent of network width, depth, or activation function, recasting it from a fit diagnostic into a structural invariant of the underlying continuous operator. For operators with infinite-dimensional kernel, $d_{eff}$ instead measures the network's finite-dimensional representational bandwidth for that kernel rather than recovering an integer invariant. Importantly, $d_{eff}$ also serves as an a priori structural diagnostic. Driving $d_{eff}$ of a well-posed problem to zero certifies that the physics and boundary constraints have absorbed the network's free directions. Building on this characterization, we introduce subspace projection strategies for boundary adaptation. Rather than retraining from scratch, we project parameter updates into the null space of the pre-trained physics operator so that new boundary conditions are satisfied without disturbing the learned physics. Gradient-based fine-tuning can match or exceed this but needs more wall-clock time and tuning, whereas subspace projection delivers near-equivalent quality in seconds to minutes. We validate on linear and nonlinear operators, demonstrating accurate adaptation to initial and boundary shifts and unencountered constraint types.

AI.tldr: PINNs suffer from task interference between physics (PDEs) and boundary conditions. The authors use Fisher Information to define $d_{eff}$, a measure of parameter directions unconstrained by the differential operator. For finite-kernel operators, $d_{eff}$ converges to the kernel dimension exactly, independent of network architecture. They propose subspace projection for boundary adaptation: project updates into the null space of the pre-trained physics operator to satisfy new BCs without retraining, achieving near-equivalent quality in seconds to minutes.
AI.motivation: PINNs struggle with task interference between satisfying PDEs and boundary conditions due to shared parameter space. The paper aims to analyze this structural conflict and provide a principled diagnostic and efficient adaptation method.
AI.method: Use Fisher Information Matrix to quantify effective degrees of freedom ($d_{eff}$) measuring parameter directions unconstrained by the differential operator. For operators with finite-dimensional kernel, $d_{eff}$ converges to kernel dimension. Propose subspace projection: when adapting to new BCs, project parameter updates into the null space of the pre-trained physics operator to preserve learned physics.
AI.result: $d_{eff}$ converges to exact kernel dimension for finite-kernel operators, independent of network width/depth/activation. For infinite-kernel operators, $d_{eff}$ measures representational bandwidth. Subspace projection achieves near-equivalent quality to fine-tuning but in seconds to minutes vs more wall-clock time.
AI.conclusion: $d_{eff}$ serves as an a priori structural diagnostic for PINN well-posedness. Subspace projection enables efficient boundary adaptation without retraining, validated on linear and nonlinear operators.

title: Addendum to Spectral bounds for the operator pencil of an elliptic system in an angle
summary: This addendum extends the spectral lower bounds established in the original paper to the Neumann problem. Under the formal positivity assumption on the elliptic tuple, we prove that the same bounds hold for Neumann boundary conditions as for the Dirichlet case.

AI.tldr: This addendum extends spectral lower bounds from Dirichlet to Neumann boundary conditions for elliptic problems under positivity assumptions.
AI.motivation: To extend previously established spectral lower bounds to the Neumann problem, verifying that the bounds hold beyond the Dirichlet case.
AI.method: The paper uses a formal positivity assumption on the elliptic tuple to prove that the same spectral lower bounds from the Dirichlet case apply to Neumann boundary conditions.
AI.result: The spectral lower bounds originally proven for Dirichlet boundary conditions also hold for Neumann boundary conditions.
AI.conclusion: Under the positivity assumption, Neumann boundary conditions yield the same spectral bounds as the Dirichlet case, generalizing the previous results.

title: Structure-Preserving Operator Splitting via JR-Decomposition for Circuit Models
summary: We investigate circuit models, namely, modified nodal analysis (MNA) in the port-Hamiltonian framework. Based on this, the JR-decomposition for the numerical treatment would offer an energy conform splitting. However, for circuit models, the application of the standard JR-decomposition is restricted. To enable a JR-decomposition for MNA, we need to relax the decomposition. To this end, we introduce the enhanced JR-decomposition, which is particularly tailored to the application to circuits. We conclude with a numerical example that illustrates the applicability of the proposed approach as well as its convergence and structure-preserving properties.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an enhanced JR-decomposition for port-Hamiltonian modified nodal analysis (MNA) circuit models, relaxing standard restrictions to enable energy-conforming numerical splitting, and demonstrates its convergence and structure-preserving properties.
AI.motivation: Standard JR-decomposition cannot be directly applied to circuit models (MNA) in the port-Hamiltonian framework, limiting numerical treatment for energy-conforming splitting.
AI.method: Introduces an enhanced JR-decomposition specifically tailored to circuit applications, relaxing the restrictions of the standard decomposition to work with MNA models.
AI.result: The proposed enhanced JR-decomposition successfully enables energy-conforming splitting for circuit models, with demonstrated convergence and structure-preserving properties via numerical example.
AI.conclusion: The enhanced JR-decomposition is applicable to circuit models and preserves both convergence and structural energy properties.

title: Wall Shear Stress Reconstruction from Concentration: Differentiable Physics and Physics-Informed Neural Networks
summary: Wall shear stress (WSS) governs near-wall transport dynamics and is a key hemodynamic indicator in cardiovascular flows, yet remains difficult to infer accurately due to the need for precise computation of near-wall velocity gradients. Passive scalar fields, such as concentration or temperature, are advected by the same underlying velocity field and have the potential to uncover hidden flow physics metrics such as WSS. In this work, we demonstrate such reconstruction from spatially limited passive scalar observations using two fundamentally different inverse frameworks: a differentiable physics framework based on discrete adjoint, PDE-constrained optimization, which enforces the governing equations as hard constraints, and physics-informed neural networks (PINNs), which treat them as soft constraints. Benchmark problems include a 2D canonical backward-facing step (2D-BFS) and a 3D patient-specific stenotic coronary artery. For the 2D-BFS case, evaluated under three measurement scenarios (near-wall, far-field, and combined), PINN achieves high accuracy when near-wall data are available but fails when restricted to far-field measurements, whereas the differentiable physics approach recovers accurate WSS across all scenarios. In the 3D patient-specific case, the differentiable physics framework outperforms PINNs, yielding accurate WSS reconstruction. These results establish that measurement location and inverse formulation jointly determine reconstruction fidelity in scalar-based near-wall flow inference. The proposed framework opens a path toward estimation of near-wall hemodynamics from scalar transport data, with broader applicability to fluid flow problems where passive scalars can be observed.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that wall shear stress (WSS) can be reconstructed from passive scalar observations using two inverse frameworks: a differentiable physics approach (discrete adjoint optimization) and physics-informed neural networks (PINNs). The differentiable physics method outperforms PINNs, especially when near-wall data are limited, in both 2D and 3D patient-specific cardiovascular flows.
AI.motivation: Wall shear stress (WSS) is a critical hemodynamic indicator in cardiovascular flows but is difficult to infer accurately because it requires precise computation of near-wall velocity gradients. The authors aim to reconstruct WSS from more easily observable passive scalar fields (like concentration or temperature) that are advected by the same flow.
AI.method: Two inverse frameworks are compared: (1) a differentiable physics framework using discrete adjoint, PDE-constrained optimization (hard constraints enforcing governing equations), and (2) physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) (soft constraints). Evaluated on 2D backward-facing step (2D-BFS) and 3D patient-specific stenotic coronary artery cases under different measurement scenarios (near-wall, far-field, combined).
AI.result: For 2D-BFS, PINN works well with near-wall data but fails with far-field data, while the differentiable physics approach recovers accurate WSS in all scenarios. In the 3D patient-specific case, the differentiable physics framework consistently outperforms PINNs for WSS reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: Measurement location and inverse formulation jointly determine reconstruction fidelity. The differentiable physics framework offers a robust path for estimating near-wall hemodynamics from scalar transport data, with broader applicability to fluid flow problems where passive scalars are observable.

title: Recovering stable kernels from exterior measurements

AI.tldr: The paper studies an inverse problem for symmetric stable operators, recovering the angular density from restricted exterior Dirichlet-to-Neumann maps, proving uniqueness results for overlapping, separated, and analytic data regimes.
AI.motivation: To recover the unknown angular density in nonlocal stable operators from partial exterior measurements, addressing an inverse problem relevant to anomalous diffusion and nonlocal PDEs.
AI.method: The paper considers restricted exterior Dirichlet-to-Neumann maps and uses three approaches: analyzing the diagonal singularity for overlapping data, exact factorization of the stable symbol for separated data with finite harmonic angular class, and analytic continuation with far-field asymptotics for real-analytic angular densities.
AI.result: Three recovery results: (1) overlapping data uniquely determines smooth elliptic angular densities; (2) separated data yields uniqueness for finite harmonic angular class; (3) separated data with analytic angular densities also yields uniqueness via analytic continuation.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes uniqueness for recovering the leading angular density in nonlocal stable operators under various measurement configurations, demonstrating that both overlapping and separated exterior data can be sufficient depending on regularity assumptions.

title: Quantum Algorithm for Nonlinear and Stochastic Homogenization via a Young-Measure based Linear Programming Formulation
summary: We study quantum algorithms for nonlinear and stochastic homogenization via a Young-measure based linear programming (LP) formulation, which lifts the nonlinear problem to a linear one in higher dimensions by treating the microscale, the gradient, and possible random variables as independent variables, thereby capturing effective macroscopic quantities without directly resolving fine-scale oscillations. The resulting LP is large but structured, and its high-dimensional nature creates regimes in which quantum LP solvers outperform direct classical solvers: in the deterministic setting, polynomial quantum speedup arises when moderate homogenized accuracy suffices; in the stochastic setting, encoding all random realizations simultaneously in a single LP yields a quantum square-root reduction in stochastic sampling cost that grows with the number of random variables. Regularity or sparsity of the Young measure may further extend these advantages to fine-scale accuracy. Numerical experiments on one- and two-dimensional benchmarks confirm the correctness of the Young-measure LP formulation.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes quantum algorithms for nonlinear/stochastic homogenization using a Young-measure LP formulation, achieving polynomial quantum speedup in deterministic settings and square-root reduction in stochastic sampling costs.
AI.motivation: To overcome the computational challenges of directly resolving fine-scale oscillations in nonlinear and stochastic homogenization problems by leveraging quantum computing advantages.
AI.method: Young-measure based LP formulation that lifts nonlinear problems to higher-dimensional linear ones, treating microscale, gradient, and random variables as independent variables, then solving with quantum LP solvers.
AI.result: Polynomial quantum speedup in deterministic settings for moderate homogenized accuracy; square-root reduction in stochastic sampling cost that grows with the number of random variables; numerical verification on 1D and 2D benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Quantum LP solvers can outperform classical solvers for homogenization problems, especially in high-dimensional regimes, with further advantages possible if Young measures are regular or sparse.

title: An inverse source problem for a fully nonlinear elliptic equation
summary:
We study an inverse source problem for fully nonlinear elliptic equations of the form
  \[
  F(D^2u)=f \quad \text{in } Ω.
  \]
  The question is whether the source term can be recovered from the Dirichlet-to-Neumann map. In two dimensions, the first linearization does not immediately give uniqueness: it leaves a natural conformal ambiguity in the linearized coefficients. For homogeneous nonlinearities $F$ with injective differential $DF$, we show that this ambiguity has a precise meaning at the level of the equation itself, namely that the source is determined up to an explicit scalar factor.
  The main point of the paper is to show how this remaining factor can be removed. We use the second linearization to extract information which is invisible at first order, and combine it with an algebraic nondegeneracy condition on the nonlinearity. Under this condition, the residual ambiguity is forced to be trivial, and the Dirichlet-to-Neumann map uniquely determines the source. The result applies, in particular, to homogeneous admissible Hessian equations of Monge--Ampère type and related examples.

AI.tldr: The paper studies inverse source problems for fully nonlinear elliptic equations, using second linearization to overcome conformal ambiguities and uniquely determine the source term from Dirichlet-to-Neumann map data.
AI.motivation: To determine whether the source term in fully nonlinear elliptic equations can be uniquely recovered from boundary measurements (Dirichlet-to-Neumann map), given that the first linearization leaves a conformal ambiguity.
AI.method: The authors use second linearization to extract information invisible at first order, combined with an algebraic nondegeneracy condition on the nonlinearity, to remove the residual ambiguity left by the first linearization.
AI.result: Under the algebraic nondegeneracy condition, the residual ambiguity is forced to be trivial, and the Dirichlet-to-Neumann map uniquely determines the source. The result applies to homogeneous admissible Hessian equations of Monge-Ampère type.
AI.conclusion: The second linearization, together with a nondegeneracy condition, uniquely determines the source term in fully nonlinear elliptic equations from boundary measurements, resolving the conformal ambiguity left by first-order analysis.

title: Error Analysis of Tr-PINNs Algorithm for 2D Incompressible Navier-Stokes Equations with Non-Homogeneous Boundary Conditions
summary: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) have been widely applied to solve Navier-Stokes equations by enforcing outputs and gradients of deep models to satisfy target equations. However, conventional PINNs only constrain the boundary terms by means of the $L^2$-norm when addressing the equations with non-homogeneous boundary conditions. This single constraint strategy may cause inaccurate boundary simulation, further resulting in the decline of prediction accuracy. To resolve this critical issue, this paper proposes an improved physics-informed neural network by correcting the error of the boundary value, which is called Tr-PINNs. Based on the results of nonhomogeneous Stokes problem, the algorithm error analysis of Tr-PINNs is established. The efficacy of the Tr-PINNs algorithm is demonstrated via numerical experiments, which further demonstrate that the Tr-PINNs algorithm achieves a remarkable improvement in computational accuracy.

AI.tldr: Tr-PINNs improves conventional PINNs by correcting boundary value errors for better accuracy when solving Navier-Stokes equations with non-homogeneous boundary conditions.
AI.motivation: Conventional PINNs use a single L²-norm constraint for boundary terms, leading to inaccurate boundary simulation and reduced prediction accuracy in non-homogeneous boundary condition problems.
AI.method: Proposes Tr-PINNs (improved physics-informed neural network) that corrects boundary value errors. Algorithm error analysis is established based on nonhomogeneous Stokes problem results.
AI.result: Numerical experiments demonstrate Tr-PINNs achieves remarkable improvement in computational accuracy compared to conventional PINNs.
AI.conclusion: Tr-PINNs effectively resolves boundary simulation inaccuracies in PINNs, significantly enhancing prediction accuracy for Navier-Stokes equations with non-homogeneous boundary conditions.

title: Large data scattering for the defocusing $k$-dispersion generalized Benjamin-Ono equation in the energy space
summary: We study the defocusing $k$-dispersion generalized Benjamin-Ono equation. For every even integer $k\geq 4$, we prove that solutions with initial data in the energy space $H^{\fracα{2}}$ are global in time and scatter. The proof combines the concentration-compactness-rigidity method of Kenig and Merle with techniques based on the Caffarelli-Silvestre extension and Tao's monotonicity formula adapted to the fractional dispersion setting.

AI.tldr: This paper proves global existence and scattering for solutions of the defocusing k-dispersion generalized Benjamin-Ono equation with initial data in the energy space H^{1/2}, for even integers k≥4, using concentration-compactness-rigidity combined with Caffarelli-Silvestre extension and Tao's monotonicity formula.
AI.motivation: To establish global well-posedness and scattering behavior for a class of fractional dispersive equations, extending the Kenig-Merle concentration-compactness framework to the fractional dispersion setting, which has applications in fluid dynamics and nonlinear wave phenomena.
AI.method: Concentration-compactness-rigidity method (Kenig-Merle) combined with Caffarelli-Silvestre extension and Tao's monotonicity formula adapted for fractional dispersion.
AI.result: For every even integer k≥4, solutions with initial data in H^{1/2} (energy space) are global in time and scatter.
AI.conclusion: The concentration-compactness-rigidity approach can be successfully adapted to fractional dispersion settings using the Caffarelli-Silvestre extension and monotonicity formulas, proving global existence and scattering for the generalized Benjamin-Ono equation.

title: DAS-PINNs for high-dimensional partial differential equations: extending deep adaptive sampling to spacetime domains
summary: Time-dependent high-dimensional partial differential equations (PDEs) with spatially localised and dynamically evolving solutions pose a fundamental challenge for physics-informed neural networks (PINNs), as uniform collocation sampling becomes increasingly ineffective in high-dimensional spatiotemporal domains. In this work, a deep adaptive sampling framework for PINNs is extended to the time-dependent setting by treating space and time as a unified domain without any explicit time marching. A normalising flow neural network model effectively learns the distribution induced by the PDE residual and generates new collocation points concentrated in regions where the solution is most difficult to learn. Unlike conventional adaptive strategies that require explicit time stepping or moving meshes, high-residual regions are automatically identified and tracked across both space and time, driven purely by the PDE residual distribution. The effectiveness of the proposed strategy is assessed on a range of benchmark problems, from sharp and moving features in two spatial dimensions to localised structures in up to eight spatial dimensions.

AI.tldr: This paper extends a deep adaptive sampling framework for PINNs to time-dependent high-dimensional PDEs by treating space and time as a unified domain, using normalizing flows to generate collocation points concentrated in high-residual regions without explicit time marching.
AI.motivation: Time-dependent high-dimensional PDEs with spatially localized and dynamically evolving solutions pose a fundamental challenge for PINNs because uniform collocation sampling becomes increasingly ineffective in high-dimensional spatiotemporal domains.
AI.method: Extends deep adaptive sampling to time-dependent PINNs by treating space and time as a unified domain; uses a normalizing flow model to learn the PDE residual distribution and generate new collocation points concentrated in high-residual regions automatically across space and time.
AI.result: Evaluated on benchmark problems from 2D sharp/moving features to localized structures in up to 8 spatial dimensions.
AI.conclusion: The adaptive sampling approach effectively identifies and tracks high-residual regions across space and time without explicit time stepping or moving meshes.

title: Homeomorphic modified wave operators for the Vlasov-Poisson system
summary: We prove modified scattering for small data solutions to the Vlasov-Poisson system in a functional framework where the initial data, scattering states, and asymptotic convergence are measured in the same topology. In addition, we show that the corresponding wave operators define homeomorphisms between the spaces of initial and scattering data, while enjoying a local Lipschitz continuity property in weaker norms. As a consequence, in the repulsive case, large spherically symmetric solutions are asymptotically stable. The proof relies in particular on the introduction of a suitable system of dynamic coordinates adapted to the asymptotic nonlinear flow.

AI.tldr: The paper proves modified scattering for small data solutions to the Vlasov-Poisson system, showing wave operators are homeomorphisms between initial and scattering data spaces, with applications to asymptotic stability of large spherically symmetric solutions in the repulsive case.
AI.motivation: To establish modified scattering results for the Vlasov-Poisson system in a unified functional framework where initial data, scattering states, and asymptotic convergence are all measured in the same topology, and to prove asymptotic stability of large spherically symmetric solutions in the repulsive case.
AI.method: The proof introduces a suitable system of dynamic coordinates adapted to the asymptotic nonlinear flow, and shows wave operators define homeomorphisms between spaces with local Lipschitz continuity in weaker norms.
AI.result: Modified scattering for small data solutions is proven; wave operators are homeomorphisms with local Lipschitz continuity; large spherically symmetric solutions are asymptotically stable in the repulsive case.
AI.conclusion: The Vlasov-Poisson system exhibits modified scattering behavior with well-structured mapping between initial and scattering data spaces, and asymptotic stability extends to large spherically symmetric solutions under repulsive interactions.

title: A q-Tsallis Safe Approximation for Chance-Constrained Programs
summary: Classical chance-constrained programs are solved by safe approximations based on the empirical CVaR, which uses a uniform measure over scenarios and systematically underweights tail events under heavy-tailed distributions. We introduce \emph{q-CCP}, a non-extensive safe approximation grounded in the Riemannian geometry of the Tsallis statistical manifold: the rank-based q-CVaR escort weights are the $g^{(q)}$-geodesic projection onto the tail simplex face, and the q-CCP feasible set is a Tsallis-divergence ball (Proposition~12). This geometric foundation yields three results. First, q-CCP is a provable strict tightening of CVaR-CCP for all $q > 1$ (Theorem~7). Second, the empirical violation ratio satisfies $ρ(q) = [1-(1-\varepsilon)^{q+1}]/\varepsilon$, independent of the tail index $ν$ (Proposition~10). Third, the feasible-region volume cost is monotone increasing in $q$ and $ν$ (Proposition~11), providing a data-adaptive safety knob. The formulation inherits convexity and coherence from the q-CVaR functional and admits an iterative LP reformulation converging in 2--3 iterations. Experiments on 15 Ibovespa equities confirm the theory (violation ratio $0.241$, $q^* = 1.50$); an M5 inventory newsvendor experiment generalises the method to supply chain ($q^* = 1.88$, cost premium $1.155\times$, zero OOS stockout violations).

AI.tldr: This paper introduces q-CCP, a non-extensive safe approximation for chance-constrained programs based on Tsallis statistics and Riemannian geometry. It proves that q-CCP is a strict tightening of CVaR-CCP for q>1, derives a tail-index-independent violation ratio formula, and demonstrates monotonic feasible-region volume cost. Experiments on Ibovespa equities and an M5 inventory newsvendor problem validate the theory.
AI.motivation: Classical chance-constrained programs using empirical CVaR systematically underweight tail events under heavy-tailed distributions, leading to inadequate risk assessment.
AI.method: The authors propose q-CCP, grounded in the Riemannian geometry of the Tsallis statistical manifold, where q-CVaR escort weights are derived as g^{(q)}-geodesic projections onto the tail simplex face, and the feasible set is a Tsallis-divergence ball.
AI.result: Three key results: (1) q-CCP strictly tightens CVaR-CCP for all q>1; (2) the empirical violation ratio ρ(q)=[1-(1-ε)^{q+1}]/ε is independent of the tail index ν; (3) the feasible-region volume cost increases monotonically with q and ν. Experiments show violation ratio 0.241 with q*=1.50 on Ibovespa equities, and q*=1.88 with cost premium 1.155x and zero OOS stockout violations in supply chain.
AI.conclusion: The q-CCP formulation inherits convexity and coherence from the q-CVaR functional, admits an efficient iterative LP reformulation converging in 2-3 iterations, and provides a data-adaptive safety knob for controlling risk in chance-constrained programs under heavy-tailed distributions.

title: Gradient descent at the Edge of Stability: free energy model and kinetic description of the two-layer network
summary: We study the dynamics of gradient descent in the Edge of Stability regime, where the learning rate is large enough to induce persistent oscillations in the loss and the sharpness. We propose a continuous-time effective model that tracks the evolution of the average trajectory coupled with the time-averaged covariance of its fast oscillations. Our analysis reveals that the natural quantity to monitor in such unstable regimes is an effective free energy, which combines the original risk functional with a curvature-related "entropic" term. Our model allows us to track the envelope of the oscillations even in situations where its dynamics evolve on similar timescales as the averaged weights. Otherwise stated, we can track the spikes that occur during the training of some neural network architectures. For wide two-layer neural networks optimized under stable non-vanishing oscillations, we derive a mean-field limit that results in a novel kinetic equation describing the joint distribution of weights and their fluctuations. We show that this equation can be interpreted as a Wasserstein-2 gradient flow of a macroscopic free energy. Finally, we provide numerical evidence on matrix factorization and deep learning tasks (CIFAR-10) to demonstrate the model's accuracy in capturing the envelope of the oscillations and the predictive power of the effective free energy.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a continuous-time effective model for gradient descent in the Edge of Stability regime, tracking both average trajectory and covariance of fast oscillations via an effective free energy, validated on matrix factorization and CIFAR-10 tasks.
AI.motivation: To understand the dynamics of gradient descent under large learning rates that cause persistent oscillations in loss and sharpness, particularly the Edge of Stability regime.
AI.method: Develops a continuous-time effective model coupling the average trajectory with time-averaged covariance of fast oscillations, introduces an effective free energy combining risk with curvature-related entropic term, and derives a mean-field limit for wide two-layer neural networks resulting in a kinetic Wasserstein-2 gradient flow equation.
AI.result: The model accurately captures the envelope of oscillations even when dynamics evolve on similar timescales as averaged weights. Numerical validation on matrix factorization and CIFAR-10 deep learning tasks confirms predictive power.
AI.conclusion: The effective free energy is a natural quantity to monitor in unstable regimes, and the proposed model can track spikes during neural network training, with the kinetic equation interpretable as a Wasserstein-2 gradient flow of macroscopic free energy.

title: Deterministic Envelopes for Tamed SGLD: Decoupling Stochastic-Gradient Noise and Localizing Taming
summary: Stochastic-gradient Langevin algorithms often use tamed denominators to stabilize non-globally Lipschitz drifts. This paper shows that when the denominator depends on the same stochastic-gradient realization as the numerator, the taming step changes the stochastic oracle itself and can create a stationary bias even if the original stochastic gradient is unbiased. We propose a structure-preserving framework for designing tamed denominators. It fixes the denominator before the oracle noise is sampled and uses localized deterministic envelopes to avoid unnecessary taming in typical regions. These kernels keep the stabilizing effect of taming while avoiding the bias introduced by a gradient-dependent denominator. Our theory explains how the stationary error splits into the bias caused by oracle-dependent taming and the remaining error introduced by deterministic stabilization. Within this deterministic-envelope family, the analysis identifies a far-tail condition that explains the limitation of local soft envelopes and motivates a hybrid member: soft in the typical region, but protected by hard-tail control on rare excursions. Experiments confirm the predicted stationary distortions of random denominators, the bias reduction of deterministic-envelope designs, and the stabilizing effect of the hybrid construction.

AI.tldr: Stochastic-gradient Langevin algorithms using tamed denominators can introduce stationary bias when the denominator depends on the same stochastic-gradient realization. The authors propose a structure-preserving framework using deterministic envelopes that fix the denominator before noise sampling, avoiding this bias while maintaining stability.
AI.motivation: To identify and resolve the stationary bias caused by gradient-dependent taming in stochastic-gradient Langevin algorithms, where the taming step inadvertently alters the stochastic oracle even when the original gradient is unbiased.
AI.method: Proposes a structure-preserving framework: (1) fix the denominator before oracle noise sampling, (2) use localized deterministic envelopes to avoid unnecessary taming in typical regions, (3) a hybrid member combining soft envelopes in typical regions with hard-tail control for rare excursions.
AI.result: Experiments confirm: predicted stationary distortions from random denominators, bias reduction using deterministic-envelope designs, and stabilizing effects of the hybrid construction.
AI.conclusion: Stationary error splits into bias from oracle-dependent taming and remaining error from deterministic stabilization. A far-tail condition explains limitations of local soft envelopes, motivating the hybrid design.
