title: Quantifying Uncertainty In Wide Two-Layer Neural Networks: On The Law Of The Limiting Fluctuation Process
summary: Uncertainty quantification in neural networks prediction is a main issue for usual applications. Our approach seeks at reducing computation costs by directly evaluating uncertainty using PDE's information on the asymptotic variance, rather than the deep ensemble method which may be seen as a Monte Carlo estimation of the prediction, requiring the training of multiple networks. We thus study the law of the limiting process describing the random fluctuations around the mean-field limit of wide two-layer neural networks trained by stochastic gradient descent in a weak-noise regime. Building on a recent trajectorial central limit theorem, in which this limit is characterized as the weak solution of a linear stochastic evolution equation, we identify its law explicitly. More precisely, we show that it is a centered Gaussian process in the dual of a weighted Sobolev space, and we derive a closed covariance representation for the finite-dimensional distributions obtained by testing it against smooth functions. This covariance is expressed through the solution of a backward transport equation with a nonlocal source term, whose coefficients are driven by the mean-field trajectory. As a consequence, by testing against the activation function at a fixed input, we obtain an expression for the limiting variance of the corresponding network-output fluctuations. We illustrate this result numerically on a one-dimensional regression example.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a computationally efficient method for uncertainty quantification in neural networks by directly evaluating asymptotic variance using PDE information, rather than expensive deep ensemble methods. The authors derive the explicit law of the limiting fluctuations of wide two-layer neural networks trained with SGD, showing it is a centered Gaussian process with closed-form covariance expressed via a backward transport equation.
AI.motivation: To reduce computation costs of uncertainty quantification in neural network predictions by replacing the expensive deep ensemble method (which requires training multiple networks) with a direct evaluation using PDE information on asymptotic variance.
AI.method: Building on a trajectorial central limit theorem, the authors characterize the limiting process of fluctuations around the mean-field limit of wide two-layer neural networks trained by SGD. They identify it as a centered Gaussian process in the dual of a weighted Sobolev space, deriving a closed covariance representation through a backward transport equation with nonlocal source terms driven by the mean-field trajectory.
AI.result: The limiting variance of network-output fluctuations is expressed by testing against the activation function at a fixed input. The approach is validated numerically on a one-dimensional regression example.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method provides a principled and efficient alternative to deep ensembles for uncertainty quantification, leveraging theoretical insights from the mean-field limit of wide neural networks and PDE-based covariance computation.

title: Mamba-Assisted Non-Markovian Closure for Reduced-Order Modeling
comment: Code will be released upon acceptance
summary: Reduced-order modeling of high-dimensional dynamical systems is often hindered by the non-Markovian closure term that represents the effect of unresolved variables on the resolved dynamics. Inspired by the Mori--Zwanzig formalism, in which the closure takes the form of a memory functional of the resolved trajectory, we recast closure modeling as a sequence modeling problem and propose the Mamba-Assisted Closure (MAC) framework: a Mamba-based sequence model, trained to predict the closure from the resolved trajectory, is coupled with the reduced-order governing equations through a numerical integrator to advance the resolved variables in time. A key feature of the framework is its exploitation of the dual representation of state-space models -- the model is trained in a sequence-to-sequence fashion via the convolutional form, and deployed for step-by-step autoregressive rollout via the recurrent form, yielding both efficient long-trajectory training and constant per-step inference cost. On the viscous Burgers' equation and the chaotic two-scale Lorenz '96 system, the MAC model substantially outperforms the Markovian reduced-order model, the GRU-based sequence model, and the Wilks method in predictive accuracy and long-time rollout stability.

AI.tldr: The MAC framework uses Mamba-based sequence models to predict non-Markovian closure terms in reduced-order modeling, achieving better accuracy and stability than Markovian, GRU, and Wilks methods on Burgers' and Lorenz '96 systems.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional dynamical systems suffer from non-Markovian closure terms when reduced-order modeling is applied, making accurate long-time prediction challenging.
AI.method: The authors cast closure modeling as a sequence modeling problem using the Mori-Zwanzig formalism, propose a Mamba-based sequence model (MAC) trained in convolutional mode and deployed in recurrent mode, coupled with numerical integrators.
AI.result: MAC substantially outperforms Markovian reduced-order model, GRU-based sequence model, and Wilks method in predictive accuracy and long-time rollout stability on viscous Burgers' equation and chaotic two-scale Lorenz '96 system.
AI.conclusion: The dual representation of state-space models (convolutional training + recurrent inference) enables efficient long-trajectory training and constant per-step cost, making MAC a promising framework for closure modeling.

title: On statistical inference for non-linear dynamical systems evolving in their global attractor
summary: We consider a two-dimensional periodic reaction-diffusion system under natural conditions on the reaction function and with initial condition $θ$. We show that on the global attractor $\mathcal A$ of the resulting dynamical system $(u_θ(t):t>0)$, a reverse Poincaré inequality holds true, and that as a consequence the map $θ\mapsto u_θ(t)$ satisfies a $L^2$-Lipschitz stability estimate on $\mathcal A$ for any $t>0$ fixed. We then show that statistical recovery of an initial condition $θ$ in the attractor $\mathcal A$, as well as prediction of the states $u_θ$, is possible from discrete measurements of the system at `fast' near parametric convergence rates.

AI.tldr: This paper proves a reverse Poincaré inequality on the global attractor of a 2D periodic reaction-diffusion system, which yields L²-Lipschitz stability of the solution map, and shows that statistical recovery of initial conditions and state prediction are possible at fast near-parametric convergence rates from discrete measurements.
AI.motivation: To establish stability and statistical recoverability of initial conditions and states in a 2D periodic reaction-diffusion system, particularly within the global attractor, under natural reaction function conditions.
AI.method: The authors prove a reverse Poincaré inequality on the global attractor 𝒜, derive L²-Lipschitz stability for the map θ↦u_θ(t) on 𝒜, and employ statistical inference techniques to achieve near-parametric convergence rates for recovery and prediction from discrete measurements.
AI.result: A reverse Poincaré inequality holds on the global attractor; the solution map is L²-Lipschitz stable on 𝒜; statistical recovery and prediction of initial conditions and states achieve near-parametric convergence rates.
AI.conclusion: The results establish that even in a complex infinite-dimensional dynamical system, the attractor enables strong stability and efficient statistical recovery/prediction at fast rates, bridging dynamical systems theory with statistical inverse problems.

title: A Two-Channel F-Transform Representation for Early Trajectory Characterization in Iterated Correlation Dynamics
summary: Many nonlinear iterative procedures generate high-dimensional trajectories whose early behavior is informative but difficult to compare directly. This paper studies a soft-computing representation problem: how to convert a short early trajectory segment into compact, interpretable, fixed-dimensional fuzzy coordinates that preserve information about subsequent convergence and trajectory geometry. The problem is investigated for iterated Pearson correlation matrices, a nonlinear matrix iteration historically connected with CONCOR-type blockmodeling and repeated-correlation methods. The proposed descriptor uses two logarithmic signals from the early post-transient regime: a step-size signal, measuring contraction magnitude, and a contraction-ratio signal, measuring local contraction evolution. Each signal is projected onto a three-node triangular fuzzy partition using zero-degree F-transform coefficients and one centered first-degree coefficient. This yields an eight-dimensional two-channel representation separating local level from local trend and contraction magnitude from contraction evolution. Across 22 matrix dimensions with 1000 trajectories per dimension, the descriptor is compared with raw trajectory samples, statistical summaries, and PCA-compressed raw features using Random Forest regression for convergence-length approximation. It achieves mean R^2 = 0.6480, close to raw trajectories (0.6518) and statistical summaries (0.6528), while improving over the step-size-only F-transform descriptor (0.5001). Repeated random-split and shifted-window experiments confirm stability. PCA and clustering further show reproducible low-dimensional organization, with the first two principal components explaining 84.26% of variance and k = 3 favored by the mean silhouette criterion.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an 8-dimensional fuzzy descriptor using F-transform coefficients of step-size and contraction-ratio signals from early trajectory segments to predict convergence behavior of nonlinear matrix iterations, achieving R²=0.648.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional nonlinear iterative trajectories have early behavior that is informative but difficult to compare directly; a compact, interpretable representation is needed.
AI.method: Two logarithmic signals (step-size and contraction-ratio) are extracted from early post-transient regime, projected onto triangular fuzzy partitions using zero-degree and first-degree F-transform coefficients, forming an 8D two-channel descriptor.
AI.result: Mean R²=0.6480 across 22 matrix dimensions (1000 trajectories each), close to raw trajectories (0.6518) and statistical summaries (0.6528), improving over step-size-only descriptor (0.5001). First two PCs explain 84.26% variance; k=3 favored by silhouette.
AI.conclusion: The fuzzy descriptor effectively preserves convergence information in a compact, interpretable form, performing nearly as well as raw data while being much smaller and interpretable.

title: Endowing variational phase-field fracture models with custom strength criteria
summary: By now, several approaches have been proposed to endow phase-field fracture models with the ability to describe crack nucleation under multiaxial stress states. These include techniques for splitting the free energy, direct modifications of the phase-field driving or resisting forces that sacrifice the variational structure of the problem, and the introduction of additional internal variables, such as plastic strains or other nonlinear strains. In this paper, we propose a fundamentally different strategy for incorporating arbitrary elastic domains into phase-field fracture models, formulated within the variational framework of generalized standard materials. The proposed approach relies on letting the dissipation potential depend on the current state of the material. In this way, the variational structure of the problem is preserved, while elastic degradation and the strength criterion remain two distinct and independently controllable aspects of the material response. Simple yet representative models are presented and thoroughly discussed to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methodology. The resulting evolution of the elastic domain is investigated in both strain and stress spaces. Moreover, numerical simulations demonstrate a range of crack nucleation processes under multiaxial loading conditions for various analytical strength surfaces. This work paves the way for future developments and applications in several directions.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a variational approach to phase-field fracture models that incorporates arbitrary elastic domains by making the dissipation potential depend on the current material state, preserving the variational structure while keeping elastic degradation and strength criteria independently controllable.
AI.motivation: Existing phase-field fracture models struggle to incorporate crack nucleation under multiaxial stress states. Current approaches (energy splitting, direct modifications of driving/resisting forces, or adding internal variables) either sacrifice the variational structure or introduce complexity. A method preserving the variational framework while allowing independent control of elastic degradation and strength criteria is needed.
AI.method: The key innovation is making the dissipation potential dependent on the current material state within the generalized standard materials variational framework. This preserves the variational structure while allowing the elastic domain and strength criterion to be independently controlled. Simple representative models are presented to demonstrate the methodology's effectiveness.
AI.result: The evolution of the elastic domain is characterized in both strain and stress spaces. Numerical simulations demonstrate successful crack nucleation under multiaxial loading conditions for various analytical strength surfaces, validating the approach's capability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach successfully incorporates arbitrary elastic domains into phase-field fracture models while preserving the variational structure. It enables independent control of elastic degradation and strength criteria, paving the way for future developments and applications in fracture modeling.

title: On Parallel and Batch-Cutting Strategies for Norm-Minimization-Based Convex Vector Optimization
summary: We develop parallel and batch-cutting variants of the norm-minimization-based outer approximation algorithm for convex vector optimization. The standard algorithm solves $N_k$ independent subproblems at each iteration~$k$ to evaluate all vertices of the current polyhedral approximation, but processes only the single best cut. We propose two improvements. First, we parallelize the \revise{subproblem evaluations} across $\nw$ workers, reducing per-iteration wall-clock time. Second, we introduce a batch-cutting strategy that adds up to $K$ supporting halfspaces per iteration, using information from all solved subproblems rather than discarding it. We prove that the batch-cutting variant inherits the convergence rate $O(k^{2/(1-q)})$ of the standard algorithm, where $k$ is the number of outer iterations and $q$ is the number of objectives. Computational experiments on eight test problems with $q \in \{2,3,4,5\}$ show that parallelism on 8 cores \revise{increases the speed by a factor of 1.1 to 4.2}, and batch cutting consistently reduces the iteration count by 62--80\%. However, the wall-clock benefit of batch cutting is problem-dependent: the additional cuts per iteration accelerate vertex count growth, so batch cutting is most effective when per-vertex subproblem cost dominates.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes parallel and batch-cutting variants of the norm-minimization-based outer approximation algorithm for convex vector optimization, achieving significant speedup through parallelism (1.1x-4.2x on 8 cores) and 62-80% iteration reduction via batch cutting, while proving the batch-cutting variant retains the same convergence rate as the standard algorithm.
AI.motivation: The standard outer approximation algorithm solves N_k independent subproblems per iteration but only uses the single best cut, wasting computational effort. The authors aim to improve efficiency by leveraging parallelism and utilizing information from all solved subproblems rather than discarding them.
AI.method: Two improvements: (1) Parallelization of subproblem evaluations across n_w workers to reduce per-iteration wall-clock time. (2) A batch-cutting strategy that adds up to K supporting halfspaces per iteration using information from all solved subproblems, with proven convergence rate O(k^{2/(1-q)}) inherited from the standard algorithm.
AI.result: Experiments on 8 test problems (q ∈ {2,3,4,5}) show: parallelism on 8 cores increases speed by 1.1x to 4.2x; batch cutting consistently reduces iteration count by 62-80%. Wall-clock benefit of batch cutting is problem-dependent; it's most effective when per-vertex subproblem cost dominates.
AI.conclusion: Both proposed variants improve upon the standard algorithm. Parallelism provides predictable speedup, while batch cutting drastically reduces iterations but its wall-clock benefit varies by problem. The batch-cutting variant maintains the same convergence rate guarantee as the standard algorithm.
